## v7.52.0 (2026-05-02) — Gemini 2.5 Flash as institutional auditor (second-pass corrector)

### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- **Architecture:** Groq (llama-3.3-70b) remains the primary signal generator. Gemini 2.5 Flash is added as a second-pass auditor that receives the Groq-generated signals and corrects specific error classes — it does not regenerate from scratch.
- **`GEMINI_URL` / `GEMINI_MODEL` constants:** `gemini-2.5-flash-preview-04-17` via `generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta` (free tier: 10 RPM, 250 RPD, 250k TPM).
- **`load_gemini_keys()`:** Reads `GEMINI_API_KEY` … `GEMINI_API_KEY_5` from env vars — same pool pattern as `load_groq_keys()`. Add keys to GitHub Actions secrets.
- **`call_gemini()`:** `generateContent` endpoint wrapper. Mirrors `call_groq` error-tag convention (`DAILY_LIMIT` / `RATE_LIMIT` / `INVALID_KEY`) for uniform handling. Temperature 0.15 (conservative — auditor must not drift). Retries on 429 with `retry-after` header or 30s default.
- **`_GEMINI_AUDIT_SYSTEM`:** Focused audit prompt (~600 tokens vs 2,400 for SIGNALS_SYSTEM_CLOSED). Two correction classes only: (1) factual errors — COT direction inversion, yield advantage inversion, carry spread wrong currency, CB rate vs hike probability confusion, directional incoherence; (2) substandard institutional language — CB bias terminology, vague editorial, first person. Explicit DO NOT CHANGE list preserves signal structure and correct theses.
- **`audit_signals_with_gemini()`:** Runs after `call_with_key_rotation` returns Groq signals. Merges corrected fields (text, title, evidence, priority) back onto original signal objects, preserving all metadata. Validates priority field against `{critical, warning, info}`. Logs count of revised signals. Full fallback on any error — audit layer never blocks publication.
- **Pipeline wiring:** Audit runs **only in `market_closed=True` mode** (pre-open weekend setups — highest hallucination risk, no live prices to anchor the LLM). Skipped in live-market mode to conserve the 250 RPD free-tier quota.
- **Failure handling:** Per-key rotation on `DAILY_LIMIT`/`RATE_LIMIT`. Non-retryable errors (400, parse failure, invalid JSON) fall back to Groq output immediately without consuming additional keys.

### Setup required
- Obtain one or more Gemini API keys from [aistudio.google.com](https://aistudio.google.com)
- Add to GitHub Actions secrets: `GEMINI_API_KEY`, optionally `GEMINI_API_KEY_2` … `GEMINI_API_KEY_5`
- No workflow YAML changes needed — keys are read at runtime

---

## v7.51.0 (2026-05-02) — Architecture: prompt compression + expanded Python post-processors

### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- **Root cause diagnosed:** `SIGNALS_SYSTEM_CLOSED` contained 17,394 chars (~4,350 tokens) with 28 constraints and 12 WRONG/CORRECT example pairs. At a 7.5:1 instruction-to-output token ratio, llama-3.3-70b was experiencing attention diffusion — later rules received less attention weight, causing inconsistent compliance. The fix is architectural: rules enforceable deterministically in Python belong in Python, not in the system prompt.
- **Prompt compression:** `SIGNALS_SYSTEM_CLOSED` reduced from 17,394 → 9,622 chars (−45%). Removed all mechanical text-transformation rules. Retained only 5 rules requiring LLM judgment: directional coherence, COT direction logic, yield advantage accuracy, carry pair accuracy, CB rate vs hike probability.
- **Expanded advisory/style stripper (Python, zero tokens):** Added 35 deterministic patterns covering the full rule set removed from the prompt: CB terminology (neutral/closed/on-hold bias → Hold bias for all 9 CB names), pair convention (JPY/EUR, JPY/GBP, JPY/AUD), investment advice (go long/short, buy the dip, sell the rally, entry point, take profit, stop loss, reduce exposure, add risk, cut positions), vague editorial (will be crucial, worth watching closely, setup is compelling), first person (we see, we view, our view).
- **Principle:** Python enforces format; LLM generates substance. This reduces Groq attention load and improves compliance on rules that require reasoning.

---

## v7.50.16 (2026-05-01) — Fix: modal sanitizer re.sub() argument order bug

### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- **Bug:** The modal uncertainty verb sanitizer introduced in v7.50.15 called
  `_re_modal.sub(pattern, string, flags=...)` instead of the correct
  `_re_modal.sub(pattern, repl, string, flags=...)` — missing the `repl` positional argument.
  Python raised `TypeError: sub() missing 1 required positional argument: 'string'`,
  which propagated as `"Groq unavailable for signals"`, causing the script to fall back to
  the cached signals.json from v7.50.13 — before any v7.50.14–15 prompt fixes were applied.
  The output therefore showed the original unfixed signals ("may compress", "BoE closed bias", etc.)
- **Fix:** Corrected argument order to `_re_modal.sub(pattern, repl, string, flags=...)`.
- **Verification:** Unit-tested all 5 patterns against the 6 actual signal texts from the
  failed run — all "may [verb]" instances correctly rewritten to "is likely to [verb]";
  "27 May" (month name) correctly preserved unchanged.

---

## v7.50.15 (2026-05-01) — Fix: modal "may/could/might" prohibition + directional coherence + CB terminology

### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- **Root cause (6 residual issues after v7.50.14):** Post-deployment audit of the v7.50.14 output
  identified 6 remaining issues across 5 of the 6 signals:
  A) Modal "may" used in 5/6 signals despite being prohibited — caused by a self-contradiction in
     the prompt: the CORRECT example on line 1515 used "may compress" while rule 1631 said "may"
     is forbidden. The LLM followed the example, not the rule.
  B) Signal 1 (USD/JPY): directional contradiction — "downside toward 155" and "upside bias" in the
     same sentence for the same pair.
  C) Signal 3 (JPY/USD): factual hallucination — "JPY's yield curve advantage" when US10Y=4.378%
     vs JGB ~1.5% means USD has the yield advantage, not JPY.
  D) Signals 2, 4, 5: THESIS → MECHANISM → CATALYST structure missing CATALYST (no date/event).
  E) Signals 4, 5: COT ≥ 30k rule violated — AUD +48.3k and GBP +32.6k not leading the thesis.
  F) Signal 5: "BoE closed bias" — non-Bloomberg terminology (correct: "BoE Hold bias").
- **Fix A — prompt self-contradiction (primary fix):** Replaced the "may compress" CORRECT example
  with a compliant "sets up downside pressure" version. Changed "Use forward-modal language: 'may'"
  instruction to "Use decisive language: 'sets up', 'likely to', 'creates risk', 'points to'".
- **Fix A — modal sanitizer (backstop):** Added Python-side post-processor that replaces
  "may/could/might + [action verb]" → "is likely to [verb]" in all closed-market signal text.
  Preserves "May" as a month name. Logs INFO when fired.
- **Fix B — directional coherence rule:** Added explicit rule with wrong/right examples showing
  that two opposite directions (downside/upside) cannot coexist in one signal for the same pair.
- **Fix C — yield advantage accuracy rule:** Added rule requiring verification of which currency
  has the higher yield from the CB RATES data block before writing "yield advantage" claims.
- **Fix D — CATALYST mandate:** Added explicit instruction in the CATALYST section requiring a
  specific date from the DATA block (not training memory) as the resolving event.
- **Fix F — CB terminology rule:** Added rule prohibiting "closed bias"; mandating Bloomberg labels
  Hold/Cut/Hike. Added to both SIGNALS_SYSTEM_CLOSED and the inline COT-OIS system prompt.

---

## v7.50.14 (2026-05-01) — Fix: SIGNALS_SYSTEM_CLOSED prompt — thesis-first structure, COT extremes, carry accuracy

### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- **Root cause (3 structural prompt failures):** The 5 non-COT-OIS signals were failing
  institutional quality standards due to three prompt deficiencies:
  (1) STYLE section provided "USD/JPY Watch" and "EUR Setup" as title examples — the LLM copied
  these generic labels instead of naming the specific driver.
  (2) No thesis-first structure was required — the "Watch for X" template was applied mechanically,
  producing headline restatements with no directional synthesis ("Watch for USD/JPY volatility",
  "Watch for EUR/USD at the open, given Trump's announcement").
  (3) COT extremes (JPY −88.5k, AUD +48.3k) were not mandated as primary signal anchors — they
  appeared only as evidence chips while the thesis sentences were driver-free.
- **Fix A — STYLE block rewritten:** Replaced generic title examples ("USD/JPY Watch", "EUR Setup",
  "BoJ — This Week") with driver-named examples ("USD/JPY — WTI / Iran Risk",
  "EUR/USD — Tariff Escalation", "USD/JPY — BoJ COT Squeeze"). Added mandatory 3-part text
  structure: (1) THESIS sentence with direction, (2) MECHANISM, (3) CATALYST. Added explicit
  WRONG/CORRECT pattern pairs showing the difference between headline restatement and a desk note.
- **Fix B — COT EXTREME MANDATORY RULE:** Any G8 currency with |COT LF net| ≥ 30,000 contracts
  must have that position as the PRIMARY THESIS of its signal. Added PAIR CONVENTION rule
  (USD/JPY not JPY/USD), FIRST PERSON FORBIDDEN ("we see"), and VAGUE EDITORIAL FORBIDDEN
  ("fundamental backdrop becoming increasingly interesting", "compelling setup").
- **Fix C — CARRY ACCURACY rule:** Carry differential cited in a signal must be between the
  two currencies in the pair. AUD/USD carry = RBA − Fed = +35bp, not AUD − JPY = +335bp.
  The LLM had cited the AUD/JPY carry spread in an AUD/USD signal.

---

## v7.50.13 (2026-05-01) — Fix: generate_signals returns only 1 signal due to misindented hike-prob guard

### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- **Root cause:** The hike/cut probability stale-training guard block (lines 5538–5567) and the
  `result.append(...)` call were at indent level 4 — OUTSIDE the `for i, s in enumerate(signals):`
  loop (indent 4 as well). Python's indentation rules mean these blocks executed once after the
  first signal iteration, then immediately hit `return result` with only 1 signal in the list.
  Signals 2–6 were silently discarded. The bug was introduced when `result.append` was moved
  outside the `for _cb` loop in v7.50.11 — the re-indent went one level too far, landing outside
  the `for i, s` loop instead of inside it.
- **Fix:** Re-indented the entire hike-prob guard block and `result.append` from indent 4 to
  indent 8, placing them correctly inside the `for i, s` loop. The token budget summary print
  and `return result` remain at indent 4 (outside the loop) — they execute once after all
  signals are processed. Verified with AST parse and indentation audit.

---

## v7.50.12 (2026-05-01) — Editorial audit: 5 prompt fixes for institutional quality

### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- **Fix 1 — SIGNALS_SYSTEM_CLOSED — "for this trade" and investment advice language:**
  Added explicit FORBIDDEN phrases list covering all prescriptive/advisory language: `"for this trade"`,
  `"go long"`, `"go short"`, `"reduce exposure"`, `"add risk"`, `"cut positions"`, `"entry point"`,
  `"take profit"`, `"stop loss"`. GUIDELINES mandate descriptive framing only. The COT-OIS signal
  was saying "catalyst for this trade" — the fix ensures signals describe conditions, not actions.
- **Fix 2 — NARRATIVE_SYSTEM_CLOSED — yield curve regime rules + USD/JPY directionality:**
  Added mandatory yield curve direction rules: bull steepener (10Y-2Y widening, long rates rising)
  is a RISK-ON signal, not risk-off. Added an explicit GEOPOLITICAL DRIVER DIRECTIONALITY rule:
  Iran tensions = USD safe-haven bid = EUR/USD down AND USD/JPY up — these cannot both be
  "pressured" by the same USD-positive catalyst. Prevents the LLM from simultaneously calling
  USD strength (EUR/USD weaker) and USD weakness (USD/JPY weaker) in the same narrative.
- **Fix 3 — DRIVERS_SYSTEM — USD pair differentiation + cross-pair carry consistency:**
  Added Rule 8a (USD PAIR DIFFERENTIATION): when multiple USD counter-pairs exist for one currency,
  each note must lead with the counter-currency's specific COT/CB/carry story, not copy-paste the
  USD macro backdrop. Added Rule 8b (CROSS-PAIR CONSISTENCY): when the same cross-pair appears under
  two currency sections (e.g. CHF/JPY under CHF and under JPY), the carry direction conclusion must
  agree — the model cannot derive contradictory spreads from the same CB rate block.
- **Fix 4 — SESSION_CONTEXT_SYSTEM_CLOSED — New York session event impact differentiation:**
  Added EVENT IMPACT DIFFERENTIATION rule: when one USD-direct release (ISM, NFP, CPI) affects
  multiple currencies, each currency's New York note must explain the pair-specific implication,
  not copy "Watch [pair] on [event]" identically for all 8 currencies. USD-direct data affects
  pairs differently: direct for EUR/USD, risk-proxy for AUD, carry context for JPY/CHF.
- **Fix 5 — SESSION_CONTEXT_SYSTEM_CLOSED — "see OIS table" and investment advice banned:**
  Added `"see OIS table"`, `"see the OIS"`, `"check the OIS"`, `"refer to OIS"` to FORBIDDEN
  phrases. Added investment advice language to FORBIDDEN list (same as Fix 1, belt-and-suspenders).
  All 5 fixes are prompt-only (no new API calls, no retries, no token budget changes).

---

## v7.50.11 (2026-05-01) — Fix: signals duplicated 8× and BoJ date hallucination in COT-OIS path

### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- **Bug 1 — 8× signal duplication:** `result.append(...)` was indented inside the `for _cb, _ccy in _CB_CCY_MAP.items():` loop (8 entries: RBNZ, RBA, BoC, BoE, ECB, BoJ, Fed/FOMC, SNB). Every signal was appended once per central bank — meaning a single Groq signal became 8 identical copies in the output. Fixed by de-denting `result.append` and its comment block from 8-space indent (inside `for _cb` loop) to 4-space indent (inside `for i, s` loop, outside `for _cb` loop). This was introduced when `result.append` was added in v7.50.10.
- **Bug 2 — LLM hallucinating "28 Apr" BoJ meeting date:** The COT-OIS dedicated system prompt told the model to "End with the next CB meeting as catalyst" without banning invented dates. The model recalled "April 28" from training data instead of the actual next meeting date supplied in the user prompt DATA section. Fixed by: (1) adding an explicit `NEVER invent or recall a date from training` rule to the output format spec in `_COT_OIS_SYSTEM`; (2) adding a `CRITICAL: Use ONLY the dates provided in the user prompt DATA section` rule at the end of `_COT_OIS_SYSTEM`; (3) making the user prompt date instruction emphatic: `use ONLY the date '{_d_next_mtg}' from the DATA above; NEVER invent a date`.

---

## v7.50.10 (2026-05-01) — Fix: signals always return [] due to missing result.append in post-processing loop

### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- **Root cause:** `generate_signals()` builds a `result = []` list and runs every signal through a post-processing loop (`for i, s in enumerate(signals)`) that applies the VIX safety net, weekend verb sanitizer, CB rate stale-training guard, and hike/cut probability guard. The loop had no `result.append(s)` at its end — it processed and discarded every signal, leaving `result` permanently empty. `generate_signals()` therefore always returned `[]` regardless of how many signals Groq produced or how many COT-OIS enforcement signals were added. This caused the entire signals path to fall into the "degenerate response" retry loop on every single run, burning through all pool keys before ultimately preserving the previous signals.json unchanged.
- **Fix:** Added `result.append({**s, "time": time_str, "text": signal_text, "title": ..., "evidence": evidence, "priority": priority})` at the end of the processing loop. The append merges all post-processed field values (sanitized text, corrected time, validated evidence, normalized priority) into the final dict. This is the pattern the loop was clearly designed to implement — the append was missing from an earlier refactor.

---

## v7.50.9 (2026-05-01) — Fix: workflow crash from invalid nonlocal + commit step abort on missing structural files

### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- **Root cause (SyntaxError):** The previous fix in v7.50.7 used `nonlocal _signals_tokens_used` inside `_tracked_call_groq()`. `nonlocal` requires a binding in an immediately enclosing *function* scope — but `_signals_tokens_used` is a module-level global, not a local variable of `generate_signals()`. Python raises `SyntaxError: no binding for nonlocal '_signals_tokens_used' found` at import time, crashing the entire script before any code runs. This caused every scheduled run to fail with exit code 1.
- **Fix:** Changed `nonlocal _signals_tokens_used` → `global _signals_tokens_used` in `_tracked_call_groq()`. The variable lives at module scope; `global` is the correct declaration for a nested function to write to it. The two other `nonlocal _cur_key_idx` usages elsewhere in the file remain correct because `_cur_key_idx` is defined as a local variable of its enclosing function.

### Engine — .github/workflows/generate-ai-narrative.yml
- **Root cause (commit step abort):** The "Commit and push" step unconditionally `cp`-ed five files — including `currency-drivers.json` and `session-context.json` — into `/tmp/ai_analysis_fresh/`. Those two files are written exclusively by `mode=structural` (i.e. `generate-structural-context.yml`, 1×/day). Since this workflow runs `mode=intraday`, those files are never written by this run. If they were absent from the checked-out public repo (first run ever, or after repo reset), `cp` exits with code 1 and the entire commit step fails — the freshly generated `index.json` and `signals.json` are never pushed.
- **Fix:** Guarded the `cp` and restore of `currency-drivers.json` and `session-context.json` with `[ -f ... ] &&` existence checks in both the initial save to `/tmp` and the per-retry restore inside the push loop. The `git add` for those two files also uses `2>/dev/null || true` so a not-yet-existing file doesn't abort staging. The three intraday-owned files (`index.json`, `signals.json`, `context_snapshot.json`) remain unconditional.

---

## v7.50.8 (2026-05-01) — Modal audit: Bloomberg-standard fixes for CB Rates and Yield Curve modals

### Frontend — assets/cb-rates-modal.js
- **Pause detection:** Added Bloomberg-standard 90-day pause threshold. When the last rate decision was >90 days ago, the Cycle trend arrow resets to `— On hold` regardless of the prior direction. This matches Bloomberg's treatment of a paused cycle as neutral, not a continuation of the last hike/cut.
- **Forward rate label:** Marked the naive ±25bp forward estimate as low-confidence (`est.`) when no OIS data is available, making it clear this is an approximation rather than a market-derived rate.

### Frontend — assets/yc-modal.js
- **10Y–2Y and 30Y–2Y spread metrics:** Added both spreads as the first two tiles in the metrics strip. Bloomberg and the IMF treat 10Y–2Y as the primary inversion indicator; 30Y–2Y is the standard long-end spread. Values in bp, colored green (positive) / red (negative), with bp change vs prior close.
- **`_ycShape` primary indicator fix:** Changed from `10Y–3M`-primary to `10Y–2Y`-primary per Bloomberg convention. The 10Y–3M spread (NY Fed recession model) is retained as a fallback when no 2Y data is available. Flat threshold tightened to ±20bp on the 10Y–2Y.

---

## v7.50.7 (2026-05-01) — Fix: signals crash with UnboundLocalError on _signals_tokens_used

### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- **Root cause:** `_tracked_call_groq()` is a nested function inside `generate_signals()`. The outer function declares `global _signals_tokens_used`, but Python's scoping rules do not propagate `global` declarations into nested functions. Because `_tracked_call_groq` contains `_signals_tokens_used += ...` (an augmented assignment), Python treats `_signals_tokens_used` as a local variable of the nested function. Reading it before the assignment completes raises `UnboundLocalError: cannot access local variable '_signals_tokens_used' where it is not associated with a value`. This caused the entire signals generation path to throw an exception on the very first `_tracked_call_groq` call, falling back to the 5 preserved previous signals every run.
- **Fix:** Added `nonlocal _signals_tokens_used` as the first line of `_tracked_call_groq`. This correctly binds the nested function's reference to the enclosing `generate_signals` scope where the variable lives, matching the pattern already used elsewhere in the file (`nonlocal _cur_key_idx` in lines 1850 and 2879).

---

## v7.50.6 (2026-05-01) — Fix: signals not regenerating due to max_tok/max_tokens parameter mismatch

### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- **Root cause:** `_tracked_call_groq()` (the internal wrapper inside `generate_signals()`) defined its token-limit parameter as `max_tok`, but every call site passed it as the keyword argument `max_tokens=`. Python raised `TypeError: got an unexpected keyword argument 'max_tokens'` on the very first signals Groq call, causing the entire signals generation path to be skipped. The system fell back to the 5 preserved signals from the previous run — signals were never refreshed.
- **Fix:** Renamed `max_tok` → `max_tokens` in the `_tracked_call_groq` signature and its internal `call_groq(...)` forwarding call (2 lines). All call sites already used `max_tokens=` correctly — no call sites required changes.

---

## v7.50.5 (2026-05-01) — Fix: create missing fetch_bond_yields.py in public repo

### Site — scripts/fetch_bond_yields.py
- **Root cause:** The `update-bond-yields` workflow (added in v7.50.0) called `python3 scripts/fetch_bond_yields.py` but the script was never created in the public repo — only the workflow was added. Every scheduled run failed immediately with `No such file or directory`.
- **Fix:** Created `scripts/fetch_bond_yields.py` (v1.0) in the public repo. The script produces exactly the 4 fields the workflow describes: `bond10y`, `bond2y`, `bond5y`, `vix` for USD and `bond10y` for EUR/GBP/JPY.
- **Sources (all public, no API key required):**
  - USD `bond10y` → `ohlc-data/us10y.json` on disk (written by `update-ohlc` at 22:30 UTC, 30 min before this workflow)
  - USD `vix` → `ohlc-data/vix.json` on disk (same)
  - USD `bond2y` → FRED public CSV endpoint (`DGS2`, no key)
  - USD `bond5y` → FRED public CSV endpoint (`DGS5`, no key)
  - EUR `bond10y` → ECB SDMX daily yield curve (`YC/B.U2.EUR.4F.G_N_A.SV_C_YM.SR_10Y`)
  - GBP `bond10y` → BOE SDIE daily CSV (`IUDMNZC` — 10-year nominal zero coupon gilt)
  - JPY `bond10y` → ECB FM SDMX monthly (`FM.M.JP.JPY.4F.BB.JP10YT_RR.YLDA` — nominal, without `R_` prefix to avoid returning real/inflation-adjusted yields)
- **Preserves existing keys:** The script loads existing `extended-data/XX.json` before writing, so non-managed fields (e.g. `consumerConfidence`, `rateMomentum`) from the engine pipeline are not overwritten.

---

## v7.50.4 (2026-05-01) — Fix: route Gold, WTI, DXY to 1H aggregation (correct session-close-date labeling)

### Engine — scripts/fetch_ohlc.py
- **Root cause (revised):** The `SESSION_OPEN_DATE_SYMBOLS` date-shift approach introduced in v7.50.3 was conceptually correct but practically unworkable: yfinance returns two bars with overlapping dates for these futures and the dedup cannot reliably distinguish the completed session bar from the partial one using 1D data alone.
- **Fix — route `CME_ICE_SYMBOLS` to `fetch_cme_ice_ohlc_from_1h()`:** Gold (`GC=F`), WTI (`CL=F`), and DXY (`DX-Y.NYB`) are now dispatched to the 1H aggregation function (already implemented in v7.50.2 for Gold, now extended to WTI and DXY). The 22:00 UTC session boundary assigns each 1H bar to the calendar date the session **closes** on — exactly matching Yahoo Finance, Bloomberg, and TradingView daily candles. The session-complete guard (`session_date <= run_date_utc`) ensures the in-progress today-bar is never written to the JSON; `dashboard.js` injects it from `quotes.json` as usual.
- **Removed dead code:** `SESSION_OPEN_DATE_SYMBOLS`, `_next_bday()` helper, and the inverted-dedup branch in the native path — all removed. The native-path dedup is now a single unconditional keep-first loop.
- **`fetch_ohlc()` docstring updated:** Three-path description: FX (28 pairs → `fetch_fx_ohlc_from_1h`), CME/ICE (3 futures → `fetch_cme_ice_ohlc_from_1h`), native 1D (8 remaining: Nikkei, SPX, Nasdaq, Stoxx, US10Y, VIX, BTC, ETH).

---

## v7.50.3 (2026-05-01) — Fix: DXY, WTI, Gold Apr 30 bar mislabeled as Apr 29 (session-open-date shift) + OHLC cache strategy fix

### Engine — scripts/fetch_ohlc.py
- **Root cause:** yfinance native 1D bars for futures that open before UTC midnight (ICE DXY at 22:00 UTC, CME WTI/Gold at 23:00 UTC) are timestamped by session **open** date, not session **close** date. The Apr 30 trading session (opened Apr 29 evening, closed Apr 30) was stored as `"2026-04-29"` in the JSON. The chart displayed it one day early. Meanwhile the `"2026-04-30"` slot contained the partial May 1 in-progress session (~30 min of data), resulting in a tiny-range stub bar on Apr 30 in the chart.
- **Fix — `SESSION_OPEN_DATE_SYMBOLS = {'dxy', 'wti', 'gold'}`:** New constant identifying symbols where yfinance uses session open date as the bar label. In the non-FX fetch loop, dates for these symbols are shifted forward by one business day via the new `_next_bday()` helper (`date + 1 day`, skipping Saturday → Monday). This maps each bar to its correct trading/close date. The last bar (partial in-progress session) is now correctly dated tomorrow, so `dashboard.js`'s strip-last-bar removes it and replaces it with live data from `quotes.json` — the existing intended design unchanged.
- **`_next_bday(d)` helper:** Added. Adds 1 calendar day, then adds 2 more if the result is Saturday (Friday session → Monday label). Sunday inputs cannot occur (futures only trade Mon–Fri).
- **Dedup comment updated:** Prior comment incorrectly described a duplicate-date scenario for DXY/WTI/Gold. After the date shift, no duplicate dates occur — dedup comment updated to reflect the actual behaviour.
- **Version bump:** script header updated to `v1.6`.

### Frontend — sw.js
- **Root cause of "no visual change" after JSON update:** `/ohlc-data/` was missing from `DATA_PATH_PREFIXES`. The service worker treated OHLC JSON files as static shell assets (cache-first strategy), so updated JSONs were never fetched from the network — the browser served stale cached versions indefinitely.
- **Fix:** `/ohlc-data/` added to `DATA_PATH_PREFIXES`. OHLC JSON files now use the network-first strategy (fresh data preferred, cache as offline fallback) — identical to all other data endpoints.
- **`CACHE_VERSION` bumped to `gi-v7.50.5`:** Forces the activate event to delete the old `gi-v7.50.4` caches across all active clients, ensuring no user is left with stale OHLC data from the incorrect cache bucket.

### Frontend — index.html
- **Version query param updated:** `dashboard.js?v=7.50.4` → `?v=7.50.5` to stay in sync with the new service worker cache version.

---

## v7.50.2 (2026-04-29) — Fix: Gold chart stub bars eliminated via CME 1H session reconstruction

### Site — scripts/fetch_ohlc.py
- **Root cause audit:** yfinance native 1D bars for `GC=F` (Gold front-month futures) had a 46% stub-bar rate — bars where `O==H` or `O==L` because Yahoo constructs them from CME settlement ticks, not from the full COMEX electronic session. This caused the Gold LW chart to render with flat candle bodies and distorted wicks, matching the appearance in the screenshot reported.
- **New function `fetch_gold_ohlc_from_1h()`:** Same hybrid 1H-aggregation pattern as `fetch_fx_ohlc_from_1h()` but with the CME COMEX session boundary: **22:00 UTC → 22:00 UTC** (17:00 ET fixed, EST/winter convention, matching Bloomberg, CME Group charts, and TradingView Gold daily candles). Hours 00–21 UTC belong to the session closing today; hours 22–23 UTC open the next session (closes tomorrow). Saturdays are dropped (CME Gold closes Friday 17:00 ET, reopens Sunday 18:00 ET). No `prev_close` open correction — Gold futures have a genuine electronic open at 18:00 ET, not a continuous market. No back-adjustment — roll gaps appear as-is (institutional front-month standard).
- **`GOLD_SYMBOLS = {'gold'}`** added as a separate routing set. `NON_FX_SYMBOLS` no longer contains `'gold'`. `FX_SYMBOLS` computed as `SYMBOLS - NON_FX_SYMBOLS - GOLD_SYMBOLS`. `fetch_ohlc()` dispatch: FX → `fetch_fx_ohlc_from_1h`, Gold → `fetch_gold_ohlc_from_1h`, everything else → native 1D.
- **WTI unaffected:** audit found only 2% stub-bar rate for `CL=F` (WTI has a cleaner Yahoo 1D feed). No change to WTI fetch path.
- **Version bump:** script header updated to `v1.4`.

---

## v7.50.1 (2026-04-29) — Fix: narrative hidden on page load due to chart scrollIntoView on boot

### Frontend — assets/dashboard.js
- **Root cause:** `loadTVChart()` called `scrollIntoView({ block: 'start' })` on `#section-fxpairs` after rendering the chart. On page load, this call is triggered by the `IntersectionObserver` in `initTVWidgets` when `#tv-chart-wrap` enters the viewport — which it does immediately in split layout. The result: `#split-upper` was always scrolled down to the chart on reload, hiding the narrative.
- **Fix 1 — `_chartAutoLoad` flag:** Added a module-level `let _chartAutoLoad = true` flag. `loadTVChart()` reads it, stores it locally as `skipScroll`, then immediately clears it to `false`. The initial auto-load skips all three `scrollIntoView` branches. All subsequent user-initiated loads (tab click, quote bar, keyboard navigation) have `_chartAutoLoad = false` and scroll normally.
- **Fix 2 — `resetSplitUpperScroll` safety net:** After `applyState()` runs in `initSplitLayout`, an IIFE explicitly sets `upper.scrollTop = 0` and repeats after 400 ms to catch any async layout shifts from the TradingView iframe loading. Ensures narrative is visible even if the browser restores scroll position from session history.

---

## v7.50.0 (2026-04-29) — Budget reclaim: migrate bond yields to public repo, disable update-extended-data

### Engine — .github/workflows/update-extended-data.yml → DISABLED
- **Audit finding:** Of the 14 fields written per currency, only `bond10y` (USD/EUR/GBP/JPY), `bond2y` (USD), `bond5y` (USD), and `vix` (USD) are consumed by any live system. The remaining 10 fields per currency (`rateMomentum`, `consumerConfidence`, `businessConfidence`, `capitalFlows`, `inflationExpectations`, `fdi`, `gdpGrowth`, `wageGrowth`, `tradeBalance`, `fdi`) are written to the repo but read by zero consumers in `dashboard.js` or `generate_narrative_signals.py`.
- **Action:** Disabled schedule trigger (`0 7 * * *` → `workflow_dispatch` only). Added institutional disable note matching the pattern already established by `update-economic-data.yml` (disabled v7.24.1). The ~10 private Actions minutes/day are reclaimed.

### Site — .github/workflows/update-bond-yields.yml → NEW
- **New public-repo workflow** runs 1×/day at 23:00 UTC (30 min after `update-ohlc` at 22:30, ensuring `ohlc-data/us10y.json` and `ohlc-data/vix.json` are on disk).
- **Produces exactly what is consumed:** `extended-data/USD.json` (bond10y, bond2y, bond5y, vix), `extended-data/EUR.json` (bond10y), `extended-data/GBP.json` (bond10y), `extended-data/JPY.json` (bond10y).
- **Zero private budget cost** — public repo, unlimited Actions minutes.
- **Canonical retry pattern** — 5-attempt hard-reset loop, linear backoff, `exit 0` on exhaustion.

### Site — scripts/fetch_bond_yields.py → NEW
- **Lightweight replacement** for `update_extended_data.py` (1590 lines → ~220 lines). Produces only the 4/14 fields that are actually consumed.
- **US 10Y / VIX:** Reads from `ohlc-data/us10y.json` / `ohlc-data/vix.json` (already on disk from `fetch_ohlc.py` / `update-ohlc`). Avoids redundant yfinance network calls.
- **US 2Y / US 5Y:** FRED public CSV endpoint (`fredgraph.csv?id=DGS2/DGS5`) — no `FRED_API_KEY` required.
- **DE 10Y:** ECB SDMX daily yield curve (`YC/B.U2.EUR.4F.G_N_A.SV_C_YM.SR_10Y`) → DBnomics/ECB fallback.
- **UK 10Y:** BOE SDIE daily (`IUDMNZC`, 10Y nominal zero coupon gilt) → DBnomics/BOE fallback.
- **JP 10Y:** ECB Financial Markets SDMX monthly nominal (`FM/M.JP.JPY.4F.BB.JP10YT_RR.YLDA`) → OECD SDMX fallback. Same sources and guards as `update_extended_data.py v13.0`.
- **Exit policy:** exits non-zero only if `USD bond10y` is unavailable — the most critical field for the LLM yield context block. All other missing fields are soft failures (logged, not fatal).

---

## v7.49.4 (2026-04-29) — Push resilience: canonical hard-reset retry pattern applied to all remaining engine workflows

### Engine — .github/workflows/update-ff-calendar.yml
- **Canonical retry loop (5 attempts, backoff, hard-reset):** Replaced `git pull --rebase` + 3-attempt loop + `exit 1` with the standard pattern: pre-copy data to `/tmp`, then for each attempt `git fetch origin main && git reset --hard origin/main`, re-apply data, commit if diff, push. Exits 0 on success or exhaustion — push collision is not a real failure; next run delivers fresh data.

### Engine — .github/workflows/update-frankfurter-cache.yml
- **Canonical retry loop:** Same fix. Uses `cp -r fx-data/ /tmp/frankfurter_fresh/` before the loop; restores directory on each attempt.

### Engine — .github/workflows/update-rates.yml
- **Canonical retry loop:** Same fix. Uses `cp -r rates/ /tmp/rates_fresh/` before the loop. Timestamp extracted before the loop so commit messages are consistent across retry attempts.

### Engine — .github/workflows/update-saxo-rr.yml
- **Full rewrite of push step:** Previous implementation used bare `git pull --rebase && git push` with no retry loop and no exit handling — a push failure would silently exit 1 (or 0 on success with no retry). Replaced with canonical 5-attempt hard-reset loop. Added timestamp to commit message for auditability.

### Engine — .github/workflows/workflow_meetings.yml
- **Canonical retry loop:** Replaced 3-attempt `git pull --rebase` loop (no sleep, exit 0 on exhaustion) with the standard 5-attempt hard-reset pattern with linear backoff. Timestamp extracted pre-loop for consistent commit messages.

---

## v7.49.3 (2026-04-29) — Rate limit resilience: RATE_LIMIT handling in session context functions + KEY_SWITCH_PAUSE_RATE constant parity

### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- **`generate_session_context` — RATE_LIMIT handling:** Key rotation loop previously only handled `DAILY_LIMIT` and exited on any other `RuntimeError` (including transient `RATE_LIMIT`). Added `elif "RATE_LIMIT"` branch: waits `KEY_SWITCH_PAUSE_RATE` (20s) and continues to the next key, matching the pattern already present in `generate_currency_drivers` and the COT-OIS loop.
- **`generate_session_context_closed` — RATE_LIMIT handling:** Same fix applied to the weekend session context key rotation loop. Also replaced the hardcoded `sleep(8)` inter-key pause with `KEY_SWITCH_PAUSE_DAILY` for consistency with all other key-rotation pauses in the script.
- **Narrative → signals inter-call pause:** Replaced hardcoded `sleep(8)` with `time.sleep(KEY_SWITCH_PAUSE_RATE)` so the pause automatically tracks `KEY_SWITCH_PAUSE_RATE = 20`. This ensures the RPM window has a meaningful backoff between the narrative and signals Groq calls on the same key.

---

## v7.49.2 (2026-04-28) — Institutional gap closure: narrative Rule 14, catalyst hierarchy, session context Rule 12, driver COT fidelity validator, signals MOVE layer

### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- **`NARRATIVE_SYSTEM` Rule 5 — CATALYST HIERARCHY:** Extended the forward-looking close rule with an explicit three-tier hierarchy (TIER 1: CB decisions; TIER 2: NFP/CPI/GDP; TIER 3: PMI/speakers). When multiple catalysts are pending, the LLM must cite the highest-impact one. When two TIER 1 events coexist within 24h, both must be named with UTC times. Includes CORRECT/WRONG examples anchored to the FOMC + BoJ same-day scenario from the failing run.
- **`NARRATIVE_SYSTEM` Rule 14 — BOND VOL + YIELD CURVE:** New rule requiring citation of MOVE Index (bond vol proxy) when above 100 or below 60, and yield curve context (2s10s inversion or sharp steepening) when relevant to CB carry pairs. MOVE > 100 signals CB rate uncertainty that elevates carry unwind risk — this is standard institutional context in a Bloomberg or JPM desk note that was absent from every narrative. Rule is additive (not a required token) — fires only at threshold levels.
- **`NARRATIVE_SYSTEM_CLOSED` — Catalyst hierarchy + news integration:** Applied the same Rule 5 catalyst hierarchy and news integration discipline to the weekend/closed-market narrative prompt. Weekend narratives now required to mine the headlines block for the actual weekend driver and reference the most informative headline as a causal clause rather than defaulting to generic CB-differential framing.
- **`SESSION_CONTEXT_SYSTEM` Rule 7 — USD note templates:** Extended the USD-note DXY-forbidden rule with four explicit correct template patterns (one per session slot: Sydney CLOSED, Tokyo CLOSED, London ACTIVE, New York UPCOMING). Eliminates the failure mode where the model knew DXY was forbidden but defaulted to "Fed on hold" boilerplate with no pair reference.
- **`SESSION_CONTEXT_SYSTEM` Rule 12 — CLOSED SESSION NEWS INTEGRATION:** New rule requiring CLOSED session notes to reflect what actually drove price in that session, not just the structural CB stance. When a headline is listed for a session in the "High-Impact Headlines by Session" block, the CLOSED note must incorporate it as a causal clause. Includes CORRECT/WRONG examples with the USD Sydney note from the failing run (the headline "Dollar Gains on Weak Stocks and Soaring Crude Prices" was the driver — the note "USD/JPY +0.11%. Fed on hold." contained zero of that information).
- **`DRIVERS_SYSTEM` Rule 4b — COT DATA FIDELITY:** New rule explicitly prohibiting the use of training-memory COT values. The failure mode was the model writing "JPY COT LF short -65.3k" (stale training value) when the CFTC data block showed lev_net=-80,578. Rule names the failure mode, gives the process (re-read the CFTC block before writing any COT reference), and includes a CORRECT/WRONG example anchored to the exact JPY values from the failing run.
- **`_sanitize_cot_values()` — Python post-processor:** New deterministic post-processing function that reads the authoritative CFTC COT value for each currency with abs(net) ≥ 30k, then regex-replaces any mismatched "CCY COT LF [direction] [value]k" pattern in the generated driver notes with the correct value. Chained after `_sanitize_driver_notes()` in the drivers generation flow. Logs corrections with old → new value for auditability.
- **`SIGNALS_SYSTEM` Rule 10 — MOVE Index as multi-layer signal:** Added "MOVE Index > 100 + CB differential + carry pair level" as an eligible combination in the multi-layer signals rule. Bond vol above 100 signals elevated rate uncertainty that compounds carry unwind risk — a layer that institutional desks (Goldman, JPM) routinely include in signals but was absent from the system.
- **`SIGNALS_SYSTEM` FORMAT — Watch level catalyst hierarchy:** Added the same three-tier catalyst hierarchy to the signals FORMAT section, governing the watch level citation in each signal's closing sentence.

---

## v7.49.1 (2026-04-28) — Narrative prompt: Rule 12 (news integration) + Rule 13 (absolute levels); session context Rule 11 (differentiation)


### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- **`NARRATIVE_SYSTEM` Rule 12 — NEWS INTEGRATION:** New mandatory rule requiring the narrative to integrate at least one headline driver into the body of every note, not only on TIER-1 lead days. Addresses the "ticker readout" failure mode where the LLM correctly cited price levels but ignored the news context explaining them. Includes process guidance (scan all headlines before writing, identify the WHY behind the strongest-moving pair), correct/wrong examples contrasting desk note style vs ticker readout, and integration style guidance (paraphrase, don't quote verbatim).
- **`NARRATIVE_SYSTEM` Rule 13 — ABSOLUTE LEVELS:** New rule requiring the narrative to cite absolute asset prices when an asset is at a historically significant level (gold above $3,000, WTI above $90 or below $60, DXY outside 95–110, FX pair near an intervention threshold). The `{{gold_pct}}` token captures daily change only — it conveys no information about whether gold is at $1,800 or $4,600. Adds mandatory citation triggers, correct/wrong examples.
- **`NARRATIVE_SYSTEM` STYLE EXAMPLES:** Added two new CORRECT examples illustrating Rules 12 and 13 (news-driven lead with absolute WTI/gold levels), and one new WRONG example explicitly showing the ticker-readout failure mode with annotation.
- **News integration post-generation validator:** Added a deterministic check after the forbidden-language check that fires when the narrative contains no keywords from the top 3 headlines. Extracts content words (≥5 chars, filtered stopword list) from headlines, checks for overlap with narrative text, and triggers a targeted retry with explicit headline quotes and Rule 12 instruction if overlap is zero. Key rotation on DAILY_LIMIT — same pattern as the existing language retry.
- **`SESSION_CONTEXT_SYSTEM` Rule 11 — SESSION DIFFERENTIATION:** New rule prohibiting cloned notes across the 4 session slots of a single currency. Names required differentiation by slot (Sydney = liquidity angle, Tokyo = Asian driver, London = most specific, New York = forward-looking), includes a concrete WRONG example (4 interchangeable generic notes) and a CORRECT example (4 genuinely differentiated notes for EUR) to anchor the expected output.

---

## v7.49.0 (2026-04-28) — Tiered Refresh Architecture: separate intraday and structural Groq key pools

### Engine — .github/workflows/generate-ai-narrative.yml
- **Scope narrowed to intraday only:** Workflow now passes `--mode intraday` to the script, generating narrative + signals exclusively. Session context and currency drivers are no longer called in this workflow.
- **Key pool reduced to keys 1–4:** Removed `GROQ_API_KEY_5` and `GROQ_API_KEY_6` from the env block — these are now reserved exclusively for the structural workflow.
- **Comment updated:** Header reflects the new tiered architecture, corrected token budget, and removed the stale "3 keys × 14,400/key" estimate that predated the 6-key setup.

### Engine — .github/workflows/generate-structural-context.yml (NEW)
- **New workflow:** Runs 1×/day at 06:00 UTC (pre-London open). Generates session context + currency drivers only via `--mode structural`.
- **Dedicated key pool — keys 5–6:** Maps `GROQ_API_KEY_5` → `GROQ_API_KEY` and `GROQ_API_KEY_6` → `GROQ_API_KEY_2` in the env block, so the existing key-rotation logic in the script operates on the structural pool without any code changes.
- **Rationale:** Session context and currency drivers depend on COT weekEnding (CFTC weekly), CB policy rates (meeting cadence), and OIS bias (weekly) — none of which change intraday. Running these 12×/day alongside narrative was consuming ~11,000 tokens/run for outputs skipped by change detection on 11 of 12 runs. Moving to 1×/day eliminates the token bleed entirely.

### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- **`--mode` flag added:** Accepts `intraday` (narrative + signals), `structural` (session context + currency drivers), or `all` (legacy / manual use). Defaults to `all` for backward compatibility with manual invocations.
- **`RUN_INTRADAY` / `RUN_STRUCTURAL` guards:** Narrative + signals blocks gated on `RUN_INTRADAY`; currency drivers + session context blocks gated on `RUN_STRUCTURAL`. No logic changes inside any block — pure execution gating.
- **Helper functions preserved at outer scope:** `_build_drivers_snapshot()` and `_drivers_inputs_changed()` remain defined unconditionally inside `main()` so they are available regardless of mode (avoids NameError if mode logic evolves).

---

## v7.48.5 (2026-04-27) — Fix FX daily OHLC: build daily bars from 1H aggregation over NY session boundary

### Engine — scripts/fetch_ohlc.py (site repo)
- **Root cause of incorrect FX candle wicks:** Yahoo Finance's native `interval="1d"` FX bars use a UTC midnight (00:00–24:00 UTC) cutoff — not a real FX session boundary. This causes the reported High/Low values to be materially wrong: intraday price extremes that occur between 17:00 NY (≈21:00 UTC) and 00:00 UTC appear in the prior day's bar, while extremes between 00:00 UTC and 17:00 NY appear in the following day's bar. The result is that candle wicks are systematically too short or too long — visible when comparing the terminal's FX daily chart against Yahoo Finance's own 4H chart (which correctly reflects intraday session ranges) or against TradingView and Bloomberg FX daily candles.
- **Industry-standard fix — 1H bar aggregation:** Added `fetch_fx_ohlc_from_1h()` which downloads 1H bars (yfinance supports up to 730 days of 1H data) and aggregates them over the correct FX session boundary: **21:00 UTC → 21:00 UTC** (≈ 17:00 New York → 17:00 New York). This is the session boundary used by Bloomberg, TradingView, and Reuters for all FX daily bar construction. The aggregated Open = first 1H bar's open, High = max of all intraday 1H highs, Low = min of all intraday 1H lows, Close = last 1H bar's close. The resulting daily bars exactly match what Yahoo Finance shows on its own 4H/1H charts.
- **Hybrid history:** yfinance caps 1H data at 730 days. For history older than 730 days, native 1D bars are used with the existing `prev_close` open correction (unchanged). The crossover is seamless — only H/L differ between the two methods, and the 1H-aggregated portion (the most recent ~2 years, which users predominantly view) is always faithful to real session ranges.
- **`fetch_ohlc()` now delegates:** All FX symbols (majors and crosses) route through `fetch_fx_ohlc_from_1h()`. Non-FX symbols (Gold, WTI, BTC, SPX, Nikkei, DXY, etc.) continue using native 1D bars, which are correct for exchange-listed assets with fixed session hours.
- **Candle open convention preserved:** After aggregation, each bar's open is set to the prior bar's close (`prev_close` convention). This guarantees candle body color always matches the daily pct sign — consistent with Bloomberg and TradingView FX daily rendering, and with the `_lwBuildTodayBar()` convention in `dashboard.js`.
- **Performance note:** Each FX symbol now makes 2 yfinance calls (1H + 1D) instead of 1. The polite `sleep(0.4)` between symbols is unchanged. Total runtime increase is bounded by the number of FX symbols (~28) × 0.4s ≈ 11s — acceptable within the workflow timeout.

---

## v7.48.4 (2026-04-27) — Frontend audit: source label accuracy and mobile VaR column fix

### Frontend — index.html
- **Myfxbook Sentiment subtitle: "real-time" → "hourly".** The `update-myfxbook-sentiment.yml` workflow runs at `:20` every hour — "real-time" overstates freshness and conflicts with Bloomberg-style source labeling conventions. Changed to `Myfxbook · hourly`.
- **Currency Strength Heatmap subtitle: added actual data sources.** Previously read "ECB daily", implying ECB is the sole source. The panel combines Stooq RT prices, yfinance, and ECB reference rates. New subtitle: `Stooq · yfinance · ECB ref rates`.
- **Reference Spreads placeholder subtitle updated.** The pre-JS skeleton said "Typical ECN · session average". The JS replaces it with the real dynamic label (vol regime + VIX + timestamp) once data loads, so the static placeholder was misleading. Changed to `ECN est. · Loading…` to accurately reflect the pre-load state.
- **VaR table: "n" column header renamed to "Days".** The bare `n` label was uninterpretable on mobile where `title=` hover tooltips don't fire. Changed to `Days` with an expanded `title` attribute. The value (e.g. 252) now reads as "252 days of history" without requiring hover.

---

## v7.48.3 (2026-04-27) — Fix Positioning Bias: column header/data mismatch across rows

### Frontend — assets/dashboard.js
- **COT-only rows now show normalised bias in col2 instead of raw LF Net contracts.** The table had a structural inconsistency: pairs with CBOE/CME data (EUR, GBP, JPY, AUD) showed ATM IV % in col1 and a normalised COT bias score in col2, while pairs without IV data (USD/CAD, USD/CHF, NZD/USD, EUR/JPY) showed raw CFTC contract counts (e.g. `-64,786`, `-8,280`) in both col1 and col2 under the same "ATM IV | COT bias" header. Mixing % units with raw contract counts in the same column under a fixed header is a data-table standards violation — Bloomberg and Eikon never do this. Fix: COT-only rows now show `—` in the ATM IV cell (column semantically correct — no index available) and the normalised `cotSkew` value (±1.5 scale, same as the ETF-IV branch) in the COT bias cell. Full LF Net and WoW Δ contract counts remain available in the per-cell hover tooltip.

---

## v7.48.2 (2026-04-27) — Fix Positioning Bias table: Direction column overflow in 220px rightpanel

### Frontend — assets/dashboard.js
- **`fmtCotNet` changed to compact K notation:** The formatter was using `.toLocaleString()`, producing values like `-64,786` and `-8,280` (6–7 characters) in the LF Net and WoW Δ columns. In the 220px `#rightpanel`, this left no width for the Direction column, clipping it entirely. Values are now formatted Bloomberg-style: `-64.8K`, `+1.2K`, `-8.3K`. The full value remains visible in the tooltip.

### Frontend — assets/dashboard.css
- **Positioning Bias table: `table-layout: fixed` with explicit column widths.** Added `table-layout: fixed; width: 100%` to the `[aria-label*="Directional positioning bias"]` table, with pinned widths: Pair 52px, col2 40px, col3 40px, Direction fills remaining space. This prevents any single column from crowding out the others regardless of content length.

---

## v7.48.1 (2026-04-27) — Fix forex-news schedule collision with update-fx-liquidity

### Engine — .github/workflows/forex-news.yml (site repo)
- **Cron changed from `'0 * * * *'` to `'5 * * * *'`:** Both `forex-news` and `update-fx-liquidity` were scheduled at `:00` UTC every hour. GitHub queues simultaneous triggers for the same concurrency group; with `cancel-in-progress: false`, the queued run blocks the next scheduled trigger, producing 30–60 min gaps between executions and occasional skipped runs (observed missing: 10:00, 11:00, 13:00, 15:00 UTC on 2026-04-27). Staggering `forex-news` to `:05` eliminates the collision entirely while keeping hourly cadence.

---

## v7.48.0 (2026-04-27) — Frontend audit: disclosure, data integrity, and NZD IV dynamic ratio

### Frontend — index.html
- **Reference Spreads panel — methodology tooltip added:** The panel title now carries a `title=` attribute explaining that values are model-derived indicative estimates (ECN floor + HV30 × vol coefficient × VIX stress multiplier), calibrated against published ECN averages, and that they are not tradeable quotes. Previously, the only disclosure was the dynamic subtitle (`ECN est. · Low vol…`); the static title gave no indication the panel was modelled. Addresses audit finding #1.

### Frontend — assets/dashboard.js
- **Myfxbook avg deposit tooltip: removed hardcoded `$96K` claim.** The tooltip for the "Average deposit" stat in the Myfxbook sentiment panel contained the literal string `'$96K average suggests the sample skews toward serious traders…'`. The displayed `avgDep` value is live from the Myfxbook API and changes over time; the tooltip comment was a snapshot that would silently become stale. Replaced with a framing-neutral statement: `'Higher average deposit values indicate more experienced or semi-professional traders — data from larger accounts carries more statistical weight.'` Addresses audit finding #2.
- **NZD/USD ATM IV proxy: replaced hardcoded `× 1.08` with dynamic HV30-derived ratio.** Both occurrences (`fetchOptionSkew` and `updatePairDetail`) previously used a fixed scalar `1.08` (NZD/AUD long-run realised-vol ratio, computed once and never updated). This ratio drifts after RBNZ or RBA rate cycles shift the relative volatility regime — e.g. the 2024–2025 RBNZ aggressive cut cycle compressed NZD realized vol relative to AUD, making 1.08 stale. The fix replaces the constant with `Math.min(1.30, Math.max(0.90, HV30['nzdusd'] / HV30['audusd']))` from `quotes.json`, updated with every intraday refresh (~5 min). This is the standard sell-side methodology (Bloomberg BVOL NZD/AUD realized vol ratio). The clamp `[0.90, 1.30]` prevents outlier distortion during flash-crash episodes. Falls back to `1.08` if `quotes.json` HV30 data is unavailable. Tooltip updated to describe the dynamic ratio methodology. Addresses audit finding #3.

### GUIDELINES.md — Sitemap
- **`guide-gbpjpy-cot.html` added to canonical sitemap.** The page existed, was indexed (`robots: index, follow`), had a canonical URL, and was present in `sitemap.xml`, but was missing from the GUIDELINES.md sitemap list. The two sources are now consistent. Addresses audit finding #4.

---



### Engine — scripts/fetch_ohlc.py (site repo)
- **Panama back-adjustment removed:** The previous session had added a proportional backward-pass adjustment (Panama method) to eliminate contract roll gaps in GC=F and CL=F. This was incorrect for a market monitoring terminal: Panama produces synthetic price levels that were never actually traded. Bloomberg `CL1 Comdty` and TradingView `CL1!` use back-adjustment only for their continuous-contract analytical instruments, explicitly documenting that prices are artificial. A monitoring terminal must show real front-month traded prices — the actual settlement price each day — exactly as Reuters Eikon, CNBC, Barchart, and Investing.com do. Concretely: WTI traded above $100 in early April 2026; Panama had rewritten those levels downward to ~$96 to make the series artificially continuous. Removed entirely.
- **HL_MAX_SPREAD guard removed for gold and wti:** The guard (`gold: 4%`, `wti: 8%`) was designed to drop dual-contract artifact bars where yfinance blends two front-month contracts into one bar with an impossible H/L range. However, during the Strait of Hormuz crisis WTI legitimately traded with 8–15% daily ranges and Gold with 4–6% daily ranges. The guard was silently dropping real trading sessions — March 2026 alone had 14 of 22 WTI bars dropped; Jan–Feb 2026 had 13 Gold bars dropped — leaving multi-day gaps in the chart. With Panama removed, any genuine artifact bar would appear as a spike, which matches how Bloomberg and TradingView handle the raw front-month series. The `dxy: 5%` guard is retained since DXY has no roll artifact pattern of this kind and genuinely cannot move 5% intraday.
- **Net result:** `fetch_ohlc.py` now delivers real, unmodified front-month OHLC prices for WTI and Gold, matching the standard of institutional data terminals. Roll gaps appear as market events (contract switch), not as data errors.

---

## v7.47.18 (2026-04-27) — Remove artificial skew1m × 0.85 decay from Positioning Bias COT fallback

### Frontend — assets/dashboard.js
- **Removed artifice:** The COT fallback mode of `fetchOptionSkew()` computed a "1M skew" column as `cotData.net * 0.85` — a fabricated time-decay coefficient with no basis in real data. This violated GUIDELINES data integrity rules ("no artificial noise in displayed data", "no estimated data without a visible label"). The ×0.85 scalar was never labeled as an estimate, so users had no way to know it was invented.
- **Replacement:** The two COT fallback columns ("1W" / "1M") are now replaced with real CFTC data: **LF Net** (current net contracts, same data already shown in the COT Positioning panel) and **WoW Δ** (week-over-week change in LF net, read from `COT_DATA_STORE`). Both values are sourced directly from the CFTC Disaggregated TFF report — no estimation, no decay coefficients.
- **Table header updated:** Fallback thead now reads "LF Net | WoW Δ | Bias" instead of "1W | 1M | Bias".
- **Panel subtitle updated:** Fallback subtitle now reads "COT · LF Net · 25d RR · Saxo" (with RR) or "COT · LF Net + WoW Δ" (without RR) instead of "COT-derived · IV unavailable".
- **Tooltips updated:** Each new column has accurate tooltips describing the real CFTC data source and field meaning — matching the COT Positioning panel tooltips.
- **Scope:** This change only affects the COT-only fallback path (ETF IV unavailable). When CBOE/CME Vol Index data is available (primary path), the table is unchanged.

---

## v7.47.17 (2026-04-27) — Fix incorrect XAUUSD=X references in fetch_ohlc.py comments

### Engine — scripts/fetch_ohlc.py (site repo)
- **Fix:** The module docstring listed `Gold (XAUUSD=X)` and the inline comment for the gold symbol entry stated `XAUUSD=X delisted on Yahoo Finance`. Both were factually wrong — the active ticker is and remains `GC=F` (CME front-month futures). Corrected both to `GC=F` with an accurate inline note. No logic, thresholds, or data changed.

## v7.47.16 (2026-04-27) — Fix CB Rate Expectations panel showing past meeting dates

### Frontend — assets/dashboard.js
- **Bug:** `fetchFedExpectations()` used `meetings?.allMeetingsFormatted?.[0]` to populate the meeting date shown in the CB Rate Expectations panel. This always returned the **first element of the full-year calendar array** (e.g. "29 Jan", "24 Jan") rather than the next upcoming meeting, because past meetings are not filtered out of `allMeetingsFormatted`. The result: all banks showed their first 2026 meeting regardless of today's date.
- **Fix:** Changed to `meetings?.nextMeeting` — the field the engine already computes correctly (filters past dates, returns the next future meeting ISO string formatted as "D Mon"). No data changes required; `meetings.json` already contained the correct `nextMeeting` values.

---

## v7.47.15 (2026-04-27) — Fix today-bar wicks missing on non-FX charts (Nikkei, Stoxx, SPX, Gold)

### Frontend — assets/dashboard.js
- **Bug:** `intradayQuote()` did not forward `high` and `low` fields from `quotes.json`. When `fetchCrossAssetData()` populated `STOOQ_RT_CACHE['nikkei']` (and stoxx, spx, gold, etc.) via `intradayQuote()`, the resulting cache object had no `high`/`low` properties. `_lwBuildTodayBar()` then fell back to `Math.max(open, close)` / `Math.min(open, close)`, producing a flat doji candle with no wicks — visible as the today-bar appearing compressed between open and close with no intraday range.
- **Fix:** Added `high` and `low` pass-through to `intradayQuote()` return value, with the same null guard used elsewhere (`q.high != null && q.high > 0`). This ensures all non-FX instruments whose cache entry comes from `intradayQuote()` (Nikkei, Stoxx, SPX, Gold, WTI, DXY, VIX) render today-bar wicks that correctly reflect the intraday session range from `quotes.json`.
- **Note:** FX pairs were unaffected — their cache entries are written by `fetchQuoteBarRT()` which already included `high`/`low`.

---

## v7.47.14 (2026-04-27) — Use smaller model for currency drivers to preserve daily token quota

### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- **Root cause of daily quota exhaustion:** Each full run consumes ~40k tokens — broken down as: Narrative (~7.5k), Signals (~9k), Session context (~5.8k), and Drivers (~17.9k). The 8 per-currency driver calls collectively account for 45% of total token consumption. With `llama-3.3-70b-versatile` on free-tier keys (~131k TPD per key), each key supports only ~3 full runs/day. Six keys = 18 runs maximum. Multiple manual workflow triggers today (debugging the BTC gap) exhausted all six keys.
- **Fix:** Added `DRIVERS_MODEL = "llama-3.1-8b-instant"` constant. All four `call_groq()` calls inside `generate_currency_drivers()` (primary, missing-pairs retry, rotation fallback, rate-limit retry) now pass `model=DRIVERS_MODEL`. The `call_groq()` function received a new optional `model` parameter (default = `MODEL`, i.e. 70b) so all narrative/signals/session-context calls are unchanged.
- **Why this works:** Driver calls produce structured JSON (short pair notes), not prose narrative — the 8b model handles this reliably. The 8b model has its own separate daily token pool on Groq free tier, so it does not draw from the 70b quota. Drivers token cost (17.9k → separate pool) frees ~45% of the 70b daily budget, increasing effective runs per key from ~3 to ~5+.
- **Unaffected:** Narrative, signals, session context, and session-context-closed all continue using `llama-3.3-70b-versatile` — quality-sensitive prose generation still uses the full model.

---
## v7.47.13 (2026-04-27) — Fix fetch_ohlc.py: end date off-by-one excludes today's and weekend bars

### Engine — scripts/fetch_ohlc.py (site repo)
- **Root cause:** yfinance's `ticker.history(start=..., end=...)` treats `end` as **exclusive** — the date passed as `end` is not included in the result. `_end = datetime.now(timezone.utc)` resolves to `2026-04-27`, so the download range was effectively `[2023-04-26, 2026-04-27)` — stopping at `2026-04-26`. Because `2026-04-26` is a Sunday and yfinance appears to require a complete UTC day to close before including it, the last bar returned was `2026-04-25` (Friday). Saturday Apr 26 and Sunday Apr 27 were both missing.
- **Fix:** Changed `_end = datetime.now(timezone.utc) + timedelta(days=1)`. With `end=2026-04-28`, the exclusive range becomes `[start, 2026-04-28)`, which includes today (Apr 27) and yesterday (Apr 26 — Saturday). The daily workflow will now deliver a complete JSON including the current partial-day bar and the prior weekend bars.
- **`dashboard.js` gap-fill (v7.47.12) remains as defense-in-depth:** Even with the corrected `end` date, there will always be a window between the last workflow run and the user's page load. The gap-fill ensures the chart shows no whitespace during that window.

---
## ~~v7.47.12~~ REVERTED — gap-fill synthetic bars violated data integrity rules

Synthetic flat-doji placeholder bars (`open=high=low=close=lastClose`) were injected into the crypto chart to cover days missing from the OHLC JSON. This is fabricated price data and violates GUIDELINES ("no estimated data without a visible label", "no `Math.random()` or invented values in displayed data"). Reverted in full. The correct fix is v7.47.13 (fetch_ohlc.py `end + 1 day`), which resolves the gap at the source. If a gap remains after the workflow runs, it is displayed as-is — accurate whitespace is preferable to invented bars.

---
## v7.47.11 (2026-04-27) — Fix BTC/ETH OHLC missing weekend bars (root cause of chart gap)

### Engine — scripts/fetch_ohlc.py
- **Root cause:** `ticker.history(period="3y", interval="1d")` applies yfinance's default NYSE/NASDAQ business-day calendar filter, which silently drops all Saturday and Sunday bars for every symbol — including BTC and ETH, which trade 24/7. The OHLC JSON files therefore had no bars for the weekend (e.g. Apr 25–26), causing the visual jump from Friday's close directly to Monday's open that appeared as a gap on the chart. The v7.47.9 LWC timestamp fix was correct (prevents LWC from collapsing weekends visually) but had nothing to render because the data was missing.
- **Fix:** Added `CRYPTO_SYMBOLS = {'btc', 'eth'}` constant. For crypto symbols, `fetch_ohlc()` now calls `ticker.history(start=..., end=..., ...)` with an explicit 3-year date range instead of `period="3y"`. yfinance only applies the business-day calendar when `period=` is used; explicit `start`/`end` passes dates directly to the Yahoo Finance API and returns all calendar days including Saturday and Sunday.
- **Non-crypto unchanged:** All FX pairs, Gold, WTI, SPX, indices continue to use `period="3y"` — weekends have no meaningful price data for those instruments.

---
## v7.47.10 (2026-04-27) — Fix drivers multi-key exhaustion and signals RPM recovery

### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py

**Bug 1 — Drivers: single rotation on DAILY_LIMIT caused EUR/GBP/JPY to always be dropped when early keys are exhausted.**
- **Root cause:** When a driver call hit `DAILY_LIMIT`, the exception handler called `_rotate_drivers_key()` once and tried the new key in a nested `try/except Exception`. If that key was *also* at the daily limit, the `RuntimeError("DAILY_LIMIT")` was caught as a generic `Exception`, printed as `"rotated-key call failed"`, and the currency was immediately skipped — even though Keys 5 and 6 were still available. In the observed log: EUR→Key2→daily, GBP→Key3→daily, JPY→Key4→daily — all three dropped because each rotated only once.
- **Fix:** Replaced the single-rotation `if rotated:` block with a `while True` loop that keeps calling `_rotate_drivers_key()` until either a key succeeds or the pool is exhausted. The inner exception handler now explicitly re-checks for `DAILY_LIMIT` in `RuntimeError` and continues the loop rather than breaking.

**Bug 2 — Signals: RPM recovery retried only the last key once after 45s.**
- **Root cause:** When all 6 keys returned `[]` (degenerate / silent RPM exhaustion), the recovery block waited 45s and retried only the very last key (`keys[-1]`). In the observed log, Key 6 was itself under RPM pressure — retrying it once with only 8s of internal `call_groq` retry produced `[]` again.
- **Fix:** Recovery now waits 60s (full RPM window) then iterates through **all** keys from Key 1 to Key 6. Earlier keys (1–4 at daily limit) are skipped cleanly via the `DAILY_LIMIT` branch; keys under RPM pressure (5–6) get a fresh attempt after the 60s window. First key that returns a non-empty result is used and returned immediately.

---
## v7.47.9 (2026-04-27) — Fix BTC/ETH weekend gap on candlestick chart

### Frontend — assets/dashboard.js
- **Root cause:** LWC v5 interprets `"YYYY-MM-DD"` string times as business-day slots and collapses Saturday/Sunday, producing a visual gap between Friday close and Monday open on BTC and ETH charts — despite the underlying OHLC JSON containing weekend bars. FX pairs correctly have no weekend bars, so this was invisible there.
- **Fix — `_CRYPTO_IDS` constant:** Added `const _CRYPTO_IDS = new Set(['btc', 'eth'])` immediately after `_LW_FX_IDS`. Documents the 24/7 continuous-session set.
- **Fix — `_renderLWChart`:** For crypto instruments, the loaded `bars` array is re-mapped before any series is created: each bar's `time` field is converted from `"YYYY-MM-DD"` to a Unix timestamp in seconds (`Date.UTC(y, m, d) / 1000`). LWC v5 detects numeric timestamps and switches to continuous (wall-clock) time mode — weekends are rendered normally, matching TradingView and Bloomberg crypto charts.
- **Fix — `_lwBuildTodayBar`:** For crypto, `timeVal` is now a Unix timestamp (seconds) instead of a `dateStr` string, ensuring the live today-bar time format matches the converted historical bars. Without this, `candleSeries.update()` would place the today-bar at a mismatched time slot.
- **Non-crypto charts unaffected:** FX pairs, Gold, WTI, SPX, Nikkei etc. continue to use `"YYYY-MM-DD"` string times — LWC business-day mode is correct for those instruments.

---

### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- `load_groq_keys()` now reads `GROQ_API_KEY_6`. Inherited by all rotation logic automatically.

### Engine — .github/workflows/generate-ai-narrative.yml
- Added `GROQ_API_KEY_6: ${{ secrets.GROQ_API_KEY_6 }}` to the `env:` block.
- **To activate:** Engine repo → Settings → Secrets → New repository secret → `GROQ_API_KEY_6` → nueva key de cuenta Groq separada.

## v7.47.7 (2026-04-27) — Fix `list index out of range` crash in drivers when all keys exhausted

### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- **Bug:** In `generate_currency_drivers()`, when all keys were exhausted mid-loop, `_rotate_drivers_key()` incremented `_cur_key_idx` beyond the pool bounds and returned `None`. On the **next loop iteration**, the line `_active_key = _key_pool[_cur_key_idx]` accessed an out-of-bounds index, raising `list index out of range`. This caused an unhandled exception that surfaced as `⚠️ Groq unavailable for currency drivers: list index out of range` in the log.
- **Fix:** Added an explicit bounds guard at the top of the per-currency loop: if `_cur_key_idx >= len(_key_pool)`, set `all_drivers[ccy] = {}` and `continue` immediately — skipping all remaining currencies without crashing. Log line: `⛔ Drivers: all keys exhausted — skipping {ccy} and remaining currencies.`
- **Observed in:** 2026-04-27 run where all 4 keys were at daily limit — drivers rotated through EUR → GBP → JPY, then attempted AUD with an exhausted index and crashed. Previous drivers were preserved correctly by the fallback, but the crash log was misleading.

---
## v7.47.6 (2026-04-27) — Per-currency scoped context for driver calls (~75% token reduction per call)

### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- **Root cause:** Each of the 8 per-currency driver calls received the full `slim_ctx` (~2,800 tokens), which includes CB rates and COT data for all 8 G8 currencies. Each call only needs data for the 2-3 currencies involved in its 7 pairs — the remaining 5-6 currencies' CB/COT lines were pure token waste.
- **New function `build_drivers_context_for_ccy(context, ccy, pairs)`:** Takes the base `slim_ctx` and strips CB rate and COT lines down to only the currencies that appear in the pair list for that call. Global sections (intraday FX prices, yields, current time) are preserved in full as they provide the intraday anchor. Sections with no per-currency lines (Retail FX Sentiment, Cross-Asset Correlation, Latest FX Headlines) are dropped entirely — they were already excluded by `build_drivers_context()` but the new function makes this explicit.
- **Wired into `generate_currency_drivers()`:** `slim_ctx` renamed to `full_slim_ctx` (built once). Each per-currency `user_prompt` now uses `scoped_ctx = build_drivers_context_for_ccy(full_slim_ctx, ccy, pairs)`. The missing-pairs retry prompt also uses `scoped_ctx`.
- **Token reduction:** CB rates section: 8 lines → ~3 lines per call. COT section: 8 lines → ~3 lines per call. Estimated per-call saving: ~250 tokens. Across 8 currencies: ~2,000 tokens/run. Across 8 active runs/day: ~16,000 tokens/day freed. This is additive to the structural change detection savings from v7.47.4.
- **Accuracy guarantee:** No data is lost — a pair note for EUR/JPY only references EUR and JPY, so BoC rate and CAD COT lines are genuinely irrelevant to that call.

---
## v7.47.5 (2026-04-27) — Add GROQ_API_KEY_5 support (5th rotation key)

### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- **Change:** `load_groq_keys()` now reads `GROQ_API_KEY_5` in addition to keys 1–4. Appended to the rotation pool; all existing `call_with_key_rotation()`, `_extra_keys`, RPM recovery, and degenerate-response guards inherit it automatically.
- **Expected impact:** With 5 keys from separate Groq accounts, the daily token budget across all accounts exceeds 500k tokens — comfortably above the 455k/day worst-case (8 active runs). Eliminates risk of 0-signal failures due to simultaneous token exhaustion across all keys.

### Engine — .github/workflows/generate-ai-narrative.yml
- **Change:** Added `GROQ_API_KEY_5: ${{ secrets.GROQ_API_KEY_5 }}` to the workflow `env:` block and updated the secrets comment header.
- **To activate:** Engine repo → Settings → Secrets and variables → Actions → New repository secret → name: `GROQ_API_KEY_5`, value: new Groq API key from a separate account. Live on next run, no redeployment needed.

## v7.47.4 (2026-04-27) — Structural change detection for currency drivers

### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- **Root cause:** Currency drivers (8 Groq calls × ~4,400 tokens each = ~35k tokens/run) were being regenerated on every run where any intraday price moved, even when their actual inputs — COT positioning, CB rates, OIS bias — had not changed. This consumed the majority of the daily token budget and left insufficient RPM headroom for signals, directly contributing to the 0-signal failures seen today.
- **Change:** Implemented structural change detection for drivers, mirroring how Bloomberg/Eikon handle currency driver panels — notes update on structural events, not on intraday pip moves. Drivers are now regenerated only when at least one of the following changes across any G8 currency: COT `weekEnding` (CFTC weekly, Fridays), CB rate `lastUpdate` (meeting decisions), OIS `bias` or `biasUpdated` (weekly via workflow_meetings.yml).
- **Implementation:** `_build_drivers_snapshot()` reads COT, rates, and meetings files and builds a per-currency fingerprint. `_drivers_inputs_changed()` compares the current fingerprint against the one stored in the previous `currency-drivers.json` payload (field `_inputs_snap`). If unchanged and existing drivers are present: `⏭ Currency drivers unchanged — preserving existing notes`. If changed: `✅ Driver inputs changed: {reason} — regenerating`. The `--force` flag bypasses the check and always regenerates.
- **Expected impact:** On typical trading days (COT static Mon–Thu, no CB meetings), drivers regenerate at most once per week (Friday after CFTC publish) plus on meeting days. This reduces driver token consumption from ~35k/run to near-zero on most runs, freeing ~280k tokens/day for narrative and signals across 8 active runs.

---
## v7.47.3 (2026-04-27) — Fix 0-signal output under simultaneous RPM pressure across all keys

### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- **Root cause:** When all 4 keys were under simultaneous RPM pressure (busy run after two consecutive forced workflows), Key 3 exhausted its 2 call_groq retries and raised RATE_LIMIT. Key 4 received the rotation but was also rate-limited — it responded without error but returned []. The empty-list guard had no extra keys left, so it returned [] immediately without any recovery wait.
- **Fix 1 — `call_groq()` retry count:** Increased from 2 attempts (1 retry) to 3 attempts (2 retries) for transient 429s. Gives each key a third chance before raising RATE_LIMIT — covers the common case where the RPM window resets within 60s.
- **Fix 2 — RPM recovery wait in `call_with_key_rotation()`:** When the empty-list guard exhausts all keys, instead of returning [] immediately, the code now waits 45s (one full RPM window) and retries once on the last key. Log line: `INFO RPM recovery wait 45s then final retry on Key N`. This directly targets the failure mode seen in this run.
- **Fix 3 — RPM wait in degenerate-response guard inside `generate_signals()`:** Before retrying with each extra key on an empty-array response, the guard now waits 30s (first extra key) to allow the RPM window to clear. Previously it used KEY_SWITCH_PAUSE_DAILY (1s) which was insufficient under simultaneous pressure.

---

### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- **Change:** `load_groq_keys()` now reads `GROQ_API_KEY_4` in addition to keys 1–3. The key is appended to the rotation pool and participates in all existing `call_with_key_rotation()`, `_extra_keys`, and intra-function rotation logic automatically — no further code changes required.

### Engine — .github/workflows/generate-ai-narrative.yml
- **Change:** Added `GROQ_API_KEY_4: ${{ secrets.GROQ_API_KEY_4 }}` to the workflow `env:` block and updated the secrets comment header. To activate: create the `GROQ_API_KEY_4` secret in the engine repo Settings → Secrets and paste the new Groq API key. The workflow picks it up on the next run without redeployment.

## v7.47.1 (2026-04-27) — Fix 0-signal output when rotated key returns degenerate empty array

### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- **Root cause:** When Key 1 hits its daily limit during the signals call and Key 2 silently returns `[]` (without raising a DAILY_LIMIT exception), the code treated the empty array as a valid response. `dropped_reasons` was also empty, so the MIN_SIGNALS retry condition (`len(filtered) < 4 and dropped_reasons`) was never satisfied, and the COT-OIS enforcement had no base signals to augment. Result: `signals.json` written with 0 alerts even when clear divergences existed (e.g. NZD/USD bilateral spread 0.926%).
- **Fix 1 — `generate_signals()` degenerate-response guard:** After `json.loads`, if `signals == []` and `_extra_keys` is non-empty, the function iterates through each extra key, re-issues the full prompt, and adopts the first non-empty result. Logs `WARNING LLM returned empty signals array — retrying with extra key N` per attempt.
- **Fix 2 — `call_with_key_rotation()` empty-list rotation:** The `generate_signals` branch now checks if the returned list is empty after a successful call. If so, it increments `key_idx`, logs `WARNING generate_signals returned [] — degenerate response. Rotating to Key N`, and loops — effectively rotating to the next key at the `call_with_key_rotation` level as a second safety net. Both fixes are needed: Fix 1 handles the case where `_extra_keys` is available inside `generate_signals`; Fix 2 catches the outer rotation if Fix 1 was not reached (e.g. during the first `call_groq` attempt before any retry logic).

---
## v7.47.0 (2026-04-27) — Fix RBNZ hike prob hallucination (100% → meetings.json value)

### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- **Root cause:** SIGNALS_SYSTEM had "RBNZ hike 100% priced" hardcoded in two example strings (NZD/USD COT-OIS signal example line 1252, EUR/NZD drivers example line 1567). The model copied those literals instead of reading `hikeProb: 40` from the OIS FORWARD BIAS block — same training-prior override pattern as the BoE rate bug (v7.46.1).
- **Fix 1 — prompt examples:** Both hardcoded `100%` occurrences replaced. NZD signal example now uses `{X}%` placeholder with an explicit ⚠️ CRITICAL note instructing the model to read the exact value from the OIS table. EUR/NZD drivers example updated to reference the OIS table generically.
- **Fix 2 — deterministic hike-prob guard:** Post-processing guard added (fires after all retries, zero Groq calls). For each signal, scans for hike/cut probability mentions per CB label (RBNZ, RBA, BoC, BoE, ECB, BoJ, Fed, SNB). Loads actual `hikeProb` from `meetings.json`. If written value diverges ≥ 15pp from actual, replaces it and logs `WARNING hike-prob guard`. Test: `100% → 40%` (60pp discrepancy) fires and corrects correctly.

---

## v7.46.3 (2026-04-27) — Fix inverted bid/ask for FX majors in fetch_intraday_quotes.py

### Frontend — scripts/fetch_intraday_quotes.py
- **Bug fix:** yfinance returns bid/ask inverted (bid > ask) for `EURUSD=X`, `GBPUSD=X`, `AUDUSD=X`, `NZDUSD=X`. Previously the validation guard rejected all four pairs, leaving `bid`/`ask` as `null` in `quotes.json`. Now the script detects the inversion, swaps bid and ask, and logs a `[swapped]` tag in the output. The 5%-of-mid proximity check still applies after the swap — genuinely bad data is still rejected. All 7/7 G8 major pairs will now produce live spread data in `quotes.json`.

## v7.46.2 (2026-04-27) — CB overlay button off by default

### Frontend — assets/dashboard.js
- **Bug fix:** `_lwShowCb` initialised to `false` on both call sites (lines 3508 and 3515) so the CB meeting overlay button is unchecked by default on chart load. Previously defaulted to `true`, causing the overlay to render on every page load without user interaction.
