## v7.39.4 (2026-04-23) — Fix: Multi-session active detection (London/NY overlap)

### Frontend — assets/heatmap-modal.js
- **Fix — Single active session wrongly suppressed NY during London/NY overlap:**
  `currentSessionName()` used a priority chain (London > New York > Tokyo > Sydney), so between
  12:00–16:00 UTC only London was highlighted; New York was never marked active even though both
  sessions are open simultaneously.
  Fix: replaced with `getActiveSessions()` which returns a `Set` of all currently open sessions.
  Both London and New York now show `●` and bold highlighting during their overlap window.
  `currentSessionName()` is retained as a legacy helper (returns the primary session for the
  fallback text only).
- **Fix — Session context notes (Groq) used same single-session logic:** The `SESSION CONTEXT`
  block was also highlighting only one session name. Updated to use the `activeSessions` Set
  so multiple sessions can appear highlighted simultaneously in the notes panel.



### Frontend — assets/heatmap-modal.js
- **Fix — UPCOMING session bars fabricated:** `populateSession()` was applying fixed weights
  (`NY=0.38, London=0.35, Tokyo=0.18, Sydney=0.09`) to ALL four sessions regardless of whether
  they had opened yet. Sessions that had not yet started received a fabricated composite value.
  Fix: introduced `sessionHasOpened()` — sessions where `utcStart > current UTC hour` (except
  Sydney, which spans midnight) render with no bar, muted opacity (0.45), and `—` value.
  The label for unopened sessions shows `· opens HH:MM` in the user's local time.
- **Fix — "UTC REFERENCE" replaced with user local timezone:** The section title, session notes
  footer, and fallback notes all displayed UTC timestamps. Changed to user's local timezone using
  `toLocaleTimeString({ timeZoneName:'short' })`, consistent with the rest of the dashboard.
  Added `utcHourToLocalStr()` and `localTzAbbr()` helper functions (mirrors the pattern in
  `dashboard.js` `utcHourToLocal()` / `tzAbbr`).

---

## v7.39.2 (2026-04-23) — Fix: USD boilerplate in counter-USD pairs + DXY/USD index in drivers

### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- **Fix — USD headline hint scope:** `_build_usd_headline_hint()` was only injected into the
  `USD` currency call, but EUR/USD, GBP/USD, AUD/USD and NZD/USD also appear in the EUR, GBP,
  AUD and NZD calls respectively. Groq received no intraday catalyst for those calls and
  defaulted to "USD steady. Fed on hold. 200bp spread anchors pair." for every counter-USD note.
  Fix: built once as `usd_hint`, injected into all 7 currencies with a USD-leg pair
  (`USD_LEG_CCYS = {EUR, GBP, AUD, CAD, CHF, NZD, USD}`). JPY is excluded — its pairs are
  all JPY-counter (USD/JPY, EUR/JPY, etc.) where the USD leg is covered by the USD call.
- **Fix — "DXY" and "USD index" added to DRIVERS_SYSTEM forbidden list:** Rule 6 now explicitly
  prohibits "DXY" and "USD index" in driver notes. USD-counter pairs must describe the USD leg
  using the pair name itself, not a proxy index. Previously forbidden only in SESSION_CONTEXT_SYSTEM.

---



### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py

- **Root cause fixed — SESSION_CONTEXT_SYSTEM:** The previous prompt contained the escape hatch
  "Only invent a catalyst if the calendar block has no event for that session + currency" which
  Groq was using to default to its training priors (BoE rate decision, RBA rate decision, etc.)
  regardless of whether those events existed in the calendar. The model's training prior for
  "what happens in London for GBP" is a rate decision — the soft instruction was insufficient
  to override it.
- **Fix — hard prohibition with explicit negative examples:** Added `CATALYST DISCIPLINE` block
  to SESSION_CONTEXT_SYSTEM listing verbatim the 7 CB rate decision patterns that are forbidden
  unless present in the calendar (`"Watch for EUR/USD on ECB rate decision"`, etc.), plus
  forbidden invented speakers (`"Fed's Powell speaks"`, `"BoJ's Ueda speaks"`). Added correct
  fallback examples for when no calendar event exists. Explicit negative examples are the most
  effective constraint for this class of hallucination.
- **Fix — user_prompt mandatory calendar rule:** Replaced soft "Use it as the primary catalyst
  source" with `MANDATORY CALENDAR RULE — NO EXCEPTIONS` block with concrete before/after
  examples using today's actual calendar events (German Flash Manufacturing PMI at 07:30 UTC,
  GBP Flash PMIs at 08:30 UTC). Removed escape hatch entirely.
- **Fix — `build_calendar_block()` medium-impact filter:** Previously excluded unreleased
  medium-impact events, causing Flash PMI speakers (medium) to not appear in the UPCOMING block.
  Removed the medium/unreleased exclusion — all high+medium events are now included in both
  released and upcoming windows.
- **Fix — empty driver dict retry:** `generate_currency_drivers()` now retries once (2s delay)
  when Groq returns an empty `{}` for a currency (CHF and USD were affected). Prevents the
  frontend from rendering a currency with no pair notes when Groq returns an empty response.

### Design rationale
LLM hallucination of UPCOMING catalysts is a training-prior problem, not a data problem — the
model knows that "London session for GBP" typically involves BoE events, so it generates them
regardless of whether they're scheduled. The fix is explicit negative examples in the system
prompt listing the exact hallucinated phrases and marking them PROHIBITED. This is the standard
approach for constraint injection: show the model what it must not generate, not just what it
should generate.

---



### Engine — .github/workflows/update-ff-calendar.yml
- **New workflow:** Fetches ForexFactory economic calendar (this week + next week) 4× daily at
  00:30, 06:30, 12:30, 20:30 UTC — aligned with major session opens. Writes normalised output
  to `calendar-data/ff_calendar.json` in the public site repo via PAT_TOKEN.
- **Concurrency guard:** `cancel-in-progress: false` — same pattern as all other data workflows.

### Engine — scripts/fetch_ff_calendar.py
- **New script:** Fetches `ff_calendar_thisweek.json` + `ff_calendar_nextweek.json` from the
  ForexFactory public API (no auth required). Normalises to internal schema: G8 currencies only
  (USD/EUR/GBP/JPY/AUD/CAD/CHF/NZD), holidays excluded, times converted from US Eastern to UTC,
  impact lower-cased (`high`/`medium`/`low`), `released` bool derived from `actual` presence.
  Deduplicates by (currency, dateISO, timeUTC, title). Idempotent: re-running on unchanged data
  produces byte-identical output (allows change detection in `generate_narrative_signals.py`).
  Preserves previous file and exits 1 if both endpoints fail — prevents corrupt overwrites.

### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- **New:** `build_calendar_block(window_hours_back=8, window_hours_ahead=24)` — reads
  `calendar-data/ff_calendar.json` and builds a session-bucketed event block. RELEASED events
  (past 8h, actual present) are grouped by the session whose UTC window contains their publish
  time. UPCOMING events (next 24h, no actual yet, high-impact only) grouped by target session.
  Block is injected into `generate_session_context()` user prompt so Groq can name specific
  catalysts (CB speakers, data releases, actual vs forecast) instead of hallucinating upcoming
  rate decisions that don't exist in the data. Falls back silently to empty string when
  `ff_calendar.json` is unavailable — news headlines block remains as secondary fallback.
- **Updated:** `generate_session_context()` — `build_calendar_block()` injected between OIS block
  and news block. User prompt updated with explicit CALENDAR/HEADLINES priority hierarchy:
  RELEASED events → cite in CLOSED/ACTIVE notes; UPCOMING events → cite in UPCOMING notes;
  hallucinate catalyst only when calendar has no entry for that session + currency.
- **Updated:** `SESSION_CONTEXT_SYSTEM` Rule 4 — UPCOMING notes must name the specific catalyst
  from the calendar block when available, using "Watch for [title]..." language.
- **Updated:** COT vs OIS DIVERGENCE rule in `SIGNALS_SYSTEM` — when speculative positioning
  contradicts the CB forward bias (COT LF net short vs OIS hike priced, or COT LF long vs OIS
  cut priced), the signal must flag this as a COT–CB divergence, not a simple positioning signal.
  Resolves the NZD/USD gap in the v7.38.0 output review where a bearish COT vs hawkish RBNZ
  divergence was not surfaced.
- **Updated:** USD driver call — injects today's top intraday headline into the USD driver user
  prompt to break the "USD steady. Fed on hold. 200bp spread anchors pair." boilerplate pattern
  that repeated across all USD pairs.

### Site — calendar-data/ff_calendar.json
- **New file:** Empty placeholder. Populated on first `update-ff-calendar.yml` run.

### Design rationale
Session context notes were hallucinating upcoming rate decisions ("Watch for EUR/USD on ECB rate
decision") for sessions where no such event existed in the data. The root cause was the absence
of a ground-truth calendar feed — Groq defaulted to the next known macro event for each currency
regardless of whether it was scheduled that day. `build_calendar_block()` provides the actual
event list so UPCOMING notes cite what is really scheduled, and CLOSED notes cite what actually
happened (with actual vs forecast). This matches Bloomberg session recap register where every
note is anchored to a verifiable catalyst.

---



### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- **New:** `build_session_news_block()` — loads `news-data/news.json`, filters to high-impact English
  headlines (same filter as the main narrative), and buckets each headline into the FX session whose
  UTC window contains its publish time (`HH:MM`). During session overlaps (London/Tokyo 07–09 UTC,
  London/NY 12–16 UTC), the highest-volume session wins (NY > London > Tokyo > Sydney). Returns a
  compact, session-labelled block (`=== High-Impact Headlines by Session ===`) capped at 4 headlines
  per session. Token cost: ~80–150 tokens/run — negligible increment.
- **Updated:** `generate_session_context()` — injects `build_session_news_block()` into the user
  prompt between the OIS bias block and the slim FX context. Groq now receives the specific catalyst
  (CB speaker, data release, geopolitical event) that occurred in each session and is instructed to
  cite it by name in CLOSED/ACTIVE notes, and as the catalyst to watch in UPCOMING notes.
- **Updated:** `SESSION_CONTEXT_SYSTEM` — added Rule 3 (CATALYST PRIORITY): when a headline is listed
  for a session, Groq must name it explicitly (CB speaker + key message, data release + direction,
  geopolitical event). Fallback to carry/CB stance only when no headline is available. This matches
  Bloomberg session recap register: "ECB's Knot: June cut not ruled out. EUR/USD bid." not generic
  "ECB on hold. Carry differential stable."

### Design rationale
Session context notes cite the catalyst that drove the session — not the structural background.
A DRIVERS note correctly cites CB stance because it explains a multi-day or multi-week differential.
A SESSION CONTEXT note correctly cites the specific speaker or data release because it explains why
the market moved *during that 8-hour window*. Separating these two data sources gives each prompt
exactly the information it needs without polluting the structural drivers with intraday noise.

---



### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- **New rule in `DRIVERS_SYSTEM`:** Materiality threshold for pair driver notes. If a pair's intraday move is < 0.20%, the note must use range-bound framing ("range-bound", "flat", "steady", "consolidating"). Fundamental attribution (CB stance, carry differential) is now reserved for moves ≥ 0.20% only. Prevents citing "RBA hike priced in" as the driver of a 0.03% micro-move.
- **`SESSION_CONTEXT_SYSTEM` improvements (from v7.36.0 follow-up):** Added prohibition on "DXY" in USD session notes — USD must reference direct pairs (EUR/USD, USD/JPY, etc.); added prohibition on "US data neutral" as a literal phrase to force per-currency framing variation in New York session notes.
- **`generate_session_context()` improvements:** Session status block (`_build_session_status_block()`) now injected into user prompt — Groq receives explicit CLOSED/ACTIVE/UPCOMING labels per session so future sessions use "Watch for..." language instead of invented recaps.

### Frontend — assets/heatmap-modal.js
- **Attribution label:** Replaced `Groq · ~5min delay` with `AI Analytics · ~5min delay` in all user-visible strings (SESSION CONTEXT footer and STRENGTH DRIVERS footer). Internal code comments unchanged. Industry standard does not expose the underlying model provider in terminal UI.

---



### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- **New:** `SESSION_CONTEXT_SYSTEM` prompt — instructs Groq to write one institutional-register note per trading session (Sydney, Tokyo, London, New York) for each G8 currency. Style matches Bloomberg session recap: session name, leading pair move, driver (CB speaker, data release, risk flow). Max 90 chars per note. Active session must be the most specific.
- **New:** `generate_session_context(api_key, context)` — makes ONE Groq call covering all 8 currencies × 4 sessions in a single JSON response. Uses the same `build_drivers_context()` slim context and `build_ois_bias_block()` OIS table used by currency drivers. Validates response: keeps only G8 currencies and known session names, truncates notes to 110 chars. Writes `ai-analysis/session-context.json`: `{ "generated_at": "...", "sessions": { "EUR": { "Sydney": "...", "Tokyo": "...", "London": "...", "New York": "..." }, ... } }`. Preserves previous file on Groq failure.
- **New:** Session context call in `main()` — runs after currency drivers with a 3s pause. Skipped when market is closed (no intraday data to describe).
- **Token impact:** ~700 tokens × 1 call = ~700 tokens/run. Adds ~1.2% to current per-run token budget.

### Engine — .github/workflows/generate-ai-narrative.yml
- Added `ai-analysis/session-context.json` to the `git add` commit line.

### Frontend — assets/heatmap-modal.js
- **New:** `_sessionCtxCache` / `_sessionCtxFetched` — fetches `session-context.json` once on first modal open, async non-blocking (mirrors `_driversCache` pattern).
- **New:** `fetchSessionContext()` — fetches `ai-analysis/session-context.json` lazily; sets `_sessionCtxCache` on success, silent fallback on failure.
- **Updated:** `populateSession()` — SESSION CONTEXT block now renders Groq-generated per-session notes when available. Format: session name in column (active highlighted in `--blue`), note text in `--font-mono`. Active session label gets `--blue` colour + filled circle indicator (`◉`). Attribution footer: `Groq · ~5min delay | UTC HH:MM`. Falls back to the previous basic intraday stats display when `session-context.json` is unavailable or not yet fetched.

---



### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- **Root cause fixed:** Groq was generating notes contradicting the OIS forward bias (e.g. "RBNZ cutting cycle" when RBNZ bias = ↑ Hike 100%) because the bias was buried in a large context block. Fix: added `build_ois_bias_block()` which reads `meetings.json` directly in Python and injects a pre-formatted, prominently-labelled OIS table at the TOP of every driver user_prompt — making it impossible to overlook.
- **Style examples corrected:** DRIVERS_SYSTEM examples now reflect actual OIS data (EUR/AUD shows "RBA hike priced in", EUR/NZD shows "RBNZ hike 100% priced"). Previous examples showed "RBA cut bets rising" which directly contradicted the data and trained Groq to do the same.
- **Explicit forbidden examples added to rules:** Added concrete ✗ examples in DRIVERS_SYSTEM Rule 3 listing exact phrases that violate the OIS data ("RBNZ cutting cycle", "RBA rate cut bets", "BoJ hike imminent") to reinforce the constraint.
- **User prompt reinforced:** Each currency call now includes an explicit reminder to check the OIS table before writing any note.

---


### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- **New:** `DRIVERS_SYSTEM` prompt — instructs Groq to write one declarative note (max 85 chars) per direct pair explaining WHY the counter-currency drove the move. Style matches Bloomberg desk note register: CB stance > COT extreme > carry > macro event. Forbidden: "amid", "as investors", "may", "could", "might". Safe-haven routing enforced (JPY/CHF only).
- **New:** `generate_currency_drivers(api_key, context)` — makes one Groq call per G8 currency (8 calls/run), returning `{ EUR: { "EUR/JPY": "...", "EUR/GBP": "...", ... }, GBP: { ... }, ... }`. Per-currency calls allow targeted pair context in the user prompt. Falls back to empty dict per currency on parse failure — frontend degrades gracefully.
- **New:** `build_drivers_context(context)` — strips retail sentiment, correlations, and headlines from context before each call. Keeps FX intraday prices, CB rates, COT, yield curve. ~40% token reduction vs full context.
- **New:** Currency drivers block in `main()` — runs after signals with 4s pause. Skipped when market is closed. Writes `ai-analysis/currency-drivers.json`: `{ "generated_at": "...", "drivers": { "EUR": { "EUR/JPY": "...", ... }, ... } }`. Preserves previous file on Groq failure.
- **Token impact:** ~220 tokens × 8 calls = ~1,760 tokens/run. Well under 1% of any single key's 500k/day free limit.

### Engine — .github/workflows/generate-ai-narrative.yml
- Added `ai-analysis/currency-drivers.json` to the `git add` commit line.

### Frontend — assets/heatmap-modal.js
- **New:** `_driversCache` / `_driversFetched` — fetches `currency-drivers.json` once on first modal open, async non-blocking.
- **Updated:** `populateCorrelations()` Strength Drivers section — each of the top-3 pair rows now shows the Groq-generated note inline below the pair name and % change, matching the mockup layout. Note is absent when data unavailable (no regression). Attribution footer: "Groq · ~5min delay".

---

## v7.34.3 (2026-04-22) — Fix: cross-asset price values incorrectly coloured green/red

### Frontend — assets/dashboard.js
- **Root cause:** `populateHeatmap()` serialised the `strengths` array via `JSON.stringify()` and embedded it directly inside the `onclick="..."` HTML attribute. `JSON.stringify` produces double-quote characters (`"`) which terminate the HTML attribute early, causing `SyntaxError: Unexpected end of input` on every heatmap refresh cycle (60s interval).
- **Fix:** Removed inline JSON. `strengths` is now stored in `window._hmStrengths` immediately before `grid.innerHTML` is set. The `onclick` attribute references `window._hmStrengths` by name — no JSON serialisation, no escaping issues. The reference is always current because `populateHeatmap()` updates `window._hmStrengths` on every call before any cell is rendered.
- **Also fixed:** Duplicate `}).join('');` closing line left over from a prior edit, which would have caused a second `SyntaxError`.

---

## v7.34.1 (2026-04-23) — Fix: heatmap-modal SyntaxError + dashboard nzdProxy ReferenceError

### Frontend — assets/heatmap-modal.js
- **Bug fix:** `SyntaxError: Identifier 'myPairs' has already been declared` — `populateSession()` declared `const myPairs` twice in the same function scope (lines 503 and 572). The second declaration (used only to compute the intraday composite for the footer note) was removed; the existing `myPairs` from line 503 is reused in its place. This prevented the entire script from loading, blocking the modal from opening.

### Frontend — assets/dashboard.js
- **Bug fix:** `ReferenceError: nzdProxy is not defined` at `updatePairDetail` line 3557 — `nzdProxy` was declared with `let` inside the `try {}` block, but referenced in the template string outside the `try` scope. Moved the declaration (`let nzdProxy = false`) to the outer function scope, before the `try` block. The assignment (`nzdProxy = true`) inside the `try` is unchanged.

---

## v7.34.0 (2026-04-22) — Feature: Currency Strength Heatmap click-through modal

### Frontend — assets/heatmap-modal.js (new file)
- **New file:** `assets/heatmap-modal.js` — institutional-grade modal opened on click of any `.hm-cell` in the Currency Strength Heatmap.
- **Pattern:** Mirrors `cb-rates-modal.js` and `corr-modal.js` exactly — IIFE, self-injecting CSS, all IDs prefixed `hm-` to avoid collisions, `z-index: 9300`.
- **Design tokens:** Uses exact site variables (`--bg #131722`, `--text #d1d4dc`, `--up #26a69a`, `--down #ef5350`, `--blue #4f7fff`, Inter + JetBrains Mono). Flag rendered via existing `flag-icons` library already loaded blocking in `index.html`.
- **Public API:** `openHeatmapModal(ccy, strengths, rtCache)`, `closeHeatmapModal()`, `hmTab(el, tabId)`.
- **Metrics strip (5 columns):** Composite %, Rank (#N/8), Pairs won (N/7), Strongest vs, Weakest vs — mirrors Bloomberg FX Strength breakdown standard.
- **Tab 1 — Pair Breakdown:** Table of 7 direct pairs showing close, prev close, % chg, currency impact with proportional bar, session range (hi–lo). Followed by full 8-currency ranking with animated horizontal bars. Impact column distinguishes pair movement from contribution to composite strength — Bloomberg/Eikon convention.
- **Tab 2 — Session:** Composite strength contribution weighted by session volume distribution (NY 38%, London 35%, Tokyo 18%, Sydney 9%). Active session highlighted in `--blue`. Note: session attribution is a volume-weighted proxy of the intraday move; exact per-session decomposition requires tick data.
- **Tab 3 — Correlations:** 8x8 strength differential matrix (row minus col composite %) with colour intensity proportional to divergence. Selected currency row/column outlined. Top 3 pairs by absolute contribution shown below. Institutional convention: strength differential matrix per Bloomberg FX monitor.
- **Footer:** `yfinance · ~5min delay · 28-pair equal-weighted model` — consistent with source label standard across all panels.
- **Accessibility:** `role="dialog"`, `aria-modal="true"`, `aria-label`, Escape key closes. Tabs carry `role="tab"` and `aria-selected`.
- **No methodology tab:** Methodology content belongs in a future `guide-fx-heatmap.html` satellite page per GUIDELINES guide-pages rule.

### Frontend — assets/dashboard.js
- **`populateHeatmap()`:** Each `.hm-cell` now has `onclick` invoking `openHeatmapModal(ccy, strengths, STOOQ_RT_CACHE)`. Added `role="button"` and `aria-label` per WCAG 4.1.2. Guard `if(window.openHeatmapModal)` ensures graceful degradation.

### Frontend — index.html
- **Script tag added:** `<script src="assets/heatmap-modal.js?v=1.0.0" defer></script>` after `yc-modal.js`.

---

## v7.33.4 (2026-04-22) — Fix: COT Positioning panel shows only base currency for crosses

### Frontend — assets/dashboard.js
- **Root cause:** `cotCcy` was resolved as `base !== 'USD' ? base : quote` — for any cross pair (neither currency is USD), this always selected only the base currency, silently ignoring the quote. The bug existed in **both** `buildInlineDetail` (sidebar crosses) and `updatePairDetail` (floating popover) independently.
- **Fix:** Introduced `isCrossPair`, `cotCcy2`, and `cotRaw2` variables in both functions. For crosses, both component currencies are now loaded from `COT_DATA_CACHE`.
- **COT layout:** Cross pairs now render two separated sub-sections, each with a currency header (e.g. "GBP · vs USD proxy" and "CHF · vs USD proxy") and a horizontal divider between them — identical to Bloomberg/Reuters terminal cross-pair COT display. Each block contains 4 metrics (LF Net, LF WoW Δ, AM Net, Net % OI) plus LF summary line. Majors unchanged.
- **Removed:** old flat 8-cell grid that interleaved both currencies without visual separation (4 per currency: LF Net, LF WoW Δ, AM Net, Net % OI) with currency tags in labels and tooltips. Majors remain unchanged (4 cells, no tag).
- **Summary bar / cotTag:** Both the inline summary bar (`buildInlineDetail`) and the popover summary (`updatePairDetail`) now show one line per component currency with LF/AM direction and aligned/diverging read for each.

---

## v7.33.3 (2026-04-22) — Fix: Rule-5 guard regex + retail divergence example in prompt

### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- **Rule-5 regex bug fixed:** Removed trailing `[-—]` from the `long%=4X/5X%` pattern — the LLM omits the separator after the percentage, causing neutral COT signals (e.g. EUR/USD net −72, long%=50%) to pass through the filter. Pattern now fires correctly on `long%=50.0%` without requiring a following dash.
- **Near-zero net guard added:** `_is_neutral_cot_signal` now performs a numeric check — any LF net with absolute value < 1,000 contracts mentioned in signal text at WARN/CRIT priority is dropped. Prevents the same class of error via a different LLM phrasing.
- **Retail divergence example:** Rule 1 in `SIGNALS_SYSTEM` now includes a concrete CORRECT/WRONG example using AUD/USD (COT LF net long +41,675 vs retail 89% short) to guide the LLM to merge the retail layer into an existing signal instead of generating a standalone retail alert.
- **Rule 10 examples:** Removed two remaining "Watch for" imperatives from the CORRECT multi-layer examples (replaced with factual/conditional phrasing). Aligns with Rule 7 prohibition added in v7.33.2.

## v7.33.2 (2026-04-22) — UX: Disclaimer relocated to panel subtitle line

### Frontend — assets/dashboard.js
- **Disclaimer placement:** "Not investment advice" appended inline to the `alerts-sub` subtitle (e.g. `5 active · AI-generated · loaded 15:21 GMT-3 · Not investment advice`). Matches Bloomberg/Reuters convention of embedding the disclaimer in the panel's metadata row rather than as a separate band.

### Frontend — index.html
- **Removed `#alerts-disclaimer` div:** Standalone disclaimer band below the panel header eliminated. Disclaimer is now carried entirely by the dynamically-set subtitle text in `dashboard.js`.

## v7.33.1 (2026-04-22) — Fix: CB Rates modal Fwd Rate always showing blank

### Frontend — assets/cb-rates-modal.js
- **Root cause:** `fwdRate` in `meetings.json` is `null` for all G8 banks — the engine field was stubbed as "future use" and never populated. `cb-rates-modal.js` used `fwdRate` directly with no fallback, displaying `—` and the static label "OIS implied" for every bank.
- **Fix:** Replicated the same Priority 1/2/3 fallback chain already present in `dashboard.js` `fetchCBExpectations()`:
  - Priority 1: explicit `fwdRate` from `meetings.json` (OIS-derived — used when the engine populates it)
  - Priority 2: probability-weighted via `cutProb`/`hikeProb` (Bloomberg WIRP standard) — computes `current + (hikeProb/100)×0.25 − (cutProb/100)×0.25`
  - Priority 3: naïve ±25bp step from `bias` field when no probability data available — prefixed with `~` to signal estimate
- **Metric cell label:** Sub-label now reads `~ est · no OIS prob` when Priority 3 is active, and `OIS implied` when Priority 1 or 2 is used. Tooltip on hover explains the source.
- **Chart forward line:** Unaffected — already used the numeric `fwdRate` variable which is now always computed.

---

## v7.33.0 (2026-04-22) — Compliance: remove prescriptive language + cross pair expansion in signals

### Frontend — index.html
- **Disclaimer added:** "For informational purposes only · Not investment advice" disclaimer line inserted as a permanent static band above `#alerts-container` in the Alerts & Market Signals panel. Mirrors Bloomberg/Eikon standard — every AI-generated output panel on institutional terminals carries this label.

### Frontend — assets/dashboard.js
- **Retail tooltip fix:** `data-tip-ex` on the Retail Client Positioning column in the Positioning Bias table was rewritten. Removed the prescriptive phrase "price tends to move against the retail crowd over time" (implied recommended action, violating GUIDELINES §No investment advice language). Replaced with a factual description of historical positioning risk without directional prescription.

### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- **Cross pair prices in context:** `FX_PAIRS_DISPLAY` expanded from 9 to 16 pairs. Added AUD/JPY, CAD/JPY, CHF/JPY (JPY crosses for carry/risk-off analysis) and EUR/CHF, GBP/CHF, AUD/CHF, NZD/CHF (CHF crosses for safe-haven demand proxy). All crosses already present in `intraday-data/quotes.json` — no new data source required.
- **Cross CB rate differentials block:** New `-- Cross CB Rate Differentials (carry context) --` section in `build_context()` computes and injects base vs quote rate differential in basis points for 9 cross pairs (GBP/JPY, EUR/JPY, AUD/JPY, CAD/JPY, CHF/JPY, EUR/CHF, GBP/CHF, AUD/CHF, NZD/CHF). Gives the LLM explicit carry math so it does not need to infer or recalculate — eliminates the inverted carry logic hallucination (SIGNALS_SYSTEM Rule 4 violation). Reads from `rates/*.json`, same source already loaded for CB Rates table.
- **Cross retail coverage logging:** Retail sentiment section now explicitly lists which cross pairs have Myfxbook data available, so the LLM knows which crosses it can use for contrarian retail signals. Removes ⚠ emoji (no emojis per GUIDELINES). Documents that NZD/CHF is absent from Myfxbook Community Outlook by source design (not a fetch error).
- **`SIGNALS_SYSTEM` prompt — cross carry rules:** Rule 2 (CB Differential) extended with explicit cross carry examples and `WRONG` patterns, mirroring the existing USD-pair examples. Rule 12 (Pair Coverage Diversity) extended with guidance on when to prioritise JPY and CHF crosses.

### Engine — scripts/fetch_saxo_rr.py
- **Cross pairs added:** `PAIRS` list expanded with GBPJPY, EURJPY, AUDJPY. These three crosses are published by Saxo Bank's FX Options Analytics page in the same 25d RR table format as USD-majors. Adding them enables the `IV/skew + COT + carry` multi-layer signal for JPY crosses (SIGNALS_SYSTEM Rule 10 preferred combination). Schema docstring updated.

- **Sidebar crosses inline detail:** Replaced `dblclick → openPairPopover()` on sidebar cross-pair rows with `click → toggleSidebarDetail()`. Clicking any `.sb-row[data-sym]` in the sidebar now expands an inline detail panel directly below the row.

This restores the full detail strip: Price group, Volatility group (HV/ATM IV/IV−HV/25d RR/Spread), COT group (dual-leg for crosses: base and quote independently), and Retail group (Myfxbook long/short %, avg entry, contrarian skew label).

## v7.31.1 (2026-04-22) — Fix: Alerts panel stuck on "Loading AI signals…"

### Frontend — assets/dashboard.js
- **Root cause:** `signals.json` is written as a bare array by `fetch_intraday_quotes.py` but as a dict `{ "generated_at": "...", "signals": [...] }` by `generate_narrative_signals.py`. When the AI narrative workflow runs after the intraday script, `signals.json` is left as a dict. `dashboard.js` checked `Array.isArray(signals)` which returns `false` for a dict, so the entire render block was skipped and the panel stayed frozen on the placeholder text.
- **Fix:** Normalise the parsed JSON before the array check — if it's a dict, extract `.signals`; if it's already an array, use it directly. Mirrors the same normalisation already applied in `fetch_intraday_quotes.py` v3.1.

---



## v7.31.0 (2026-04-22) — ATM IV source upgraded to CBOE/CME FX Volatility Indexes

### Summary
Replaces the entire ATM IV source cascade with the institutional standard: the CBOE/CME FX Volatility Index family (^EUVIX, ^BPVIX, ^JYVIX, ^AUDVIX). These indexes use the same variance-swap replication methodology as the VIX, are published jointly by CBOE and CME, and are the underlying benchmark for Bloomberg BVOL FX. The previous CME futures options and CBOE ETF option chain approach was unreliable because yfinance returns `impliedVolatility=0.0` for illiquid FX ETF option chains and 404/no-chain for CME continuous futures. Under the new cascade, EUR, GBP, JPY, and AUD resolve from the CBOE/CME index unconditionally; CHF and CAD fall through to CME options/ETF as before; NZD is derived from ^AUDVIX × 1.08.

### Engine — scripts/fetch_intraday_quotes.py (v3.1 → v3.2)
- **New source 0 in IV cascade:** `_iv_from_cboe_index()` fetches `regularMarketPrice` from `^EUVIX`, `^BPVIX`, `^JYVIX`, `^AUDVIX` via yfinance. Index value IS the annualised IV in percentage points — no option chain parsing required.
- **`_CBOE_FX_VOL_INDEX` map added:** `eurusd→^EUVIX`, `gbpusd→^BPVIX`, `usdjpy→^JYVIX`, `audusd→^AUDVIX`.
- **NZD coverage extended:** `nzdusd` now derived from `^AUDVIX × 1.08` (long-run NZD/AUD realised vol ratio). Labeled `est. CBOE ^AUDVIX × 1.08`. Previously: no IV at all when CME/ETF failed.
- **Cascade order:** `CBOE/CME index → CME front-month options → CME IMM quarterly → CBOE ETF`. Previous primary (CME options) becomes fallback.
- **Source label in output:** `"source": "CBOE ^EUVIX"` etc. (instead of `"CME 6E=F options"` or `"FXE"`).
- **Expected resolution:** 6/7 pairs from CBOE index (EUR, GBP, JPY, AUD direct; NZD derived); CHF and CAD from CME/ETF fallback. Previous result was 0/6 pairs resolved.

### Frontend — dashboard.js
- **ATM IV tooltip (mini-panel):** Updated source description from CBOE FX ETF options to CBOE/CME FX Volatility Indexes.
- **ATM IV tooltip (pair detail full panel):** Three tooltip variants updated — majors direct, NZD proxy (now `^AUDVIX × 1.08`), and cross triangulation (now `CBOE/CME vol index values`).
- **Positioning Bias panel header:** `ETF IV · CBOE · 25d RR · Saxo` → `CBOE/CME Vol · 25d RR · Saxo`.
- **td1Body tooltip (positioning table):** Updated to reference CBOE/CME FX Volatility Index + VIX methodology.
- **td1Ex disclaimer:** Updated from ETF liquidity caveat to CBOE/CME index delay note (~15min) and CHF/CAD fallback disclosure.
- **SOURCE 1 comment block:** Updated from "ETF IV (primary)" to "CBOE/CME Vol Index (primary)".

### Docs — about.html
- **Data sources table:** Row `Positioning Bias (CME IV · COT · 25d RR)` → `Positioning Bias (CBOE/CME Vol · COT · 25d RR)`. Source column updated to list ^EUVIX/^BPVIX/^JYVIX/^AUDVIX as primary with CME options and ETF as fallbacks.
- **Estimated data section:** NZD IV description updated from "derived from AUD options" to "derived from the AUD/USD CBOE/CME vol index (^AUDVIX)".

### Docs — guide-dashboard.html
- **Positioning Bias section h3:** `CME IV · COT · 25d RR` → `CBOE/CME Vol · COT · 25d RR`.
- **Panel mock subtitle chip:** `CME IV · CME/ETF · 25d RR · Saxo` → `CBOE/CME Vol · 25d RR · Saxo`.
- **Positioning Bias description paragraph:** Full rewrite of ATM IV source description — replaces CME 6E/6B/6J/6A + ETF fallback narrative with CBOE/CME FX Vol Index explanation including methodology note (variance-swap replication, Bloomberg BVOL benchmark), coverage map, and CHF/CAD/NZD fallback disclosure.

### Docs — GUIDELINES.md
- **ATM IV source label:** `ETF options IV · CBOE` → `CBOE/CME Vol Index (^EUVIX/^BPVIX/^JYVIX/^AUDVIX)` with CHF/CAD/NZD fallback cascade documented.
- **Version footer bumped:** v7.27.1 → v7.31.0.

### Version strings — about.html, guide-dashboard.html
- **Version string bumped:** v7.30.8 → v7.31.0.

---

## v7.30.8 (2026-04-22) — CorrSignal signals.json dict/list normalization fix

### Engine — scripts/fetch_intraday_quotes.py
- **Bug fix — `[CorrSignal] Error: 'str' object has no attribute 'get'`:** `signals.json` is written by `generate_narrative_signals.py` as a dict `{ "generated_at": "...", "signals": [...] }`, but the CorrSignal block assumed a bare list. When iterating a dict in Python, values are the string keys (`"generated_at"`, `"signals"`), so `s.get("source")` raised `AttributeError`. Fix: normalize the loaded JSON — if dict, extract `["signals"]`; if list, use directly. Added a secondary guard (`isinstance(s, dict)`) in the list-comprehension filter to defensively drop any non-dict entries regardless of source.
- **Script version bumped to v3.1.**

---

## v7.30.7 (2026-04-22) — ATM IV source migrated from CBOE ETFs to CME FX futures options

### Engine — scripts/fetch_intraday_quotes.py
- **Root cause:** yfinance returns `impliedVolatility=0.0` for CBOE FX ETF options (FXE, FXB, FXY, FXA, FXF, FXC) when the chain is illiquid — bid/ask = 0 at ATM strikes. All six pairs were failing the 3%–40% plausibility gate and writing `null` to `quotes.json`, causing ATM IV and IV−HV to show `—` in the terminal.
- **Fix — CME FX futures options as primary source:** Replaced `fetch_fx_etf_iv()` with a new implementation that queries CME FX futures option chains (`6E=F`, `6B=F`, `6J=F`, `6A=F`, `6S=F`, `6C=F`) as the primary source. CME FX futures options are the institutional benchmark for FX implied volatility with daily open interest exceeding 400,000 contracts on 6E alone, pricing continuously during US/EU session hours and tracking OTC interbank vol within 0.5–1 vol point for majors.
- **Candidate cascade per pair:** (1) continuous front-month (`6E=F`) — most liquid; (2) next IMM quarterly (`6EM26`, `6EU26`) — reliable when front-month rolls thin; (3) CBOE ETF fallback (`FXE`, etc.) — retained for edge-case continuity.
- **IMM quarterly contract generation** (`_imm_contracts()`): dynamically builds Mar/Jun/Sep/Dec contract codes (H/M/U/Z) for any root symbol, forward-looking from current date.
- **Shared ATM extraction helper** (`_atm_iv_from_ticker()`): consolidated IV extraction logic — spot resolution, expiry selection (≥4 days), ATM strike, call→put fallback, decimal→percent conversion, plausibility gate — used by all candidates to eliminate code duplication.
- **Source label in `quotes.json`** reflects actual source used: `"CME 6E=F options"` vs `"FXE options"`, allowing monitoring of fallback frequency.
- **Log summary** reports CME vs ETF sourced count: `[FX-IV] 6/6 pairs resolved (6 via CME, 0 via ETF)`.
- **`test_cme_fx_options.py`** retained in repo as diagnostic tool; production logic is now in the main script.

---

- **CSS filter settled:** `invert(0.90) hue-rotate(180deg) brightness(0.93)` — reduced invert to 0.90 to lower blue tint from hue-rotate, brightness at 0.93 for balanced luminance match to `#131722`.

## v7.30.1 (2026-04-20) — Forbidden-language Python guard for narrative

### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- **Forbidden-language detector (new):** After the first Groq call, `_forbidden_hits()` scans the narrative for words/phrases explicitly banned in NARRATIVE_SYSTEM Rule 2: "simmer", "creeping in", "lingers", "looms", "on the back of", "amid", "as investors digest", "market participants", "cautious tone". If any are found, a targeted retry is triggered with the exact violations listed and a reminder of the correct action-verb alternatives.
- **`NARRATIVE_FORBIDDEN` patterns** moved to the top of `generate_narrative()` alongside `MIXED_FORBIDDEN`, with `_forbidden_hits()` helper function. The duplicate `import re as _re` inside the function was consolidated.
- **Unit tests:** 4 cases pass — the two exact failing narratives from the last two runs are correctly detected; clean institutional narratives are correctly passed through.

---

## v7.30.0 (2026-04-20) — NARRATIVE_SYSTEM institutional rewrite

### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- **Rule 2 — Declarative language (new):** Explicit forbidden-words list: "simmer", "creeping in", "lingers", "looms", "weighs on", "on the back of", "amid", "as investors digest", "market participants", "cautious tone". Replaced with a list of correct action verbs: retreats, firms, bids, sells off, holds, breaks, compresses, widens, unwinds. CORRECT/WRONG examples included.
- **Rule 3 — Safe-haven routing (new):** In risk-off regimes, safe-haven flows go to JPY, CHF, and Gold — not EUR or GBP. EUR/USD moves in risk-off must be attributed to CB differential or positioning, never "safe-haven demand". Directly addresses the recurring "EUR/USD holds on safe-haven demand" error.
- **Rule 5 — Forward-looking close (new):** The final sentence of every narrative must name the next catalyst: upcoming CB meeting, scheduled data release, technical level, or intervention threshold. Examples: "Watch BoJ meeting Apr 28"; "NFP Friday is the next USD directional catalyst."
- **Rule 6 — Lead variation (new):** Prohibits always opening with the same geopolitical/USD/JPY structure. Defines 4 lead types by session driver: geopolitical, CB/macro, vol/positioning, quiet session. The WRONG example is the exact phrase the model had been repeating: "Geopolitical tensions simmer as Trump comments on Iran..."
- **Rule 10 — Regime badge note:** Added explicit instruction that the regime label appears as a visual badge on the terminal — do not repeat it in the narrative text; convey it implicitly through language and drivers.
- **Style examples updated:** All 3 CORRECT examples now show a forward-looking close. Added explicit WRONG example at the bottom with the exact phrase pattern to avoid.

---

## v7.29.6 (2026-04-20) — signals.json metadata + stale indicator + generation timestamp in panel

### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- **signals.json format change:** File now written as `{"generated_at": "...", "signals": [...]}` instead of a plain array. Backward-compatible: old plain-array format still reads correctly on first run after deploy.
- **Preserved signals path updated:** `existing_signals` loader handles both old (array) and new (object) formats.

### Engine — .github/workflows/generate-ai-narrative.yml
- **Bug fix — echo step:** Workflow summary step now handles new `{generated_at, signals}` object format. `data.get('signals', data)` extracts the array with fallback to the legacy plain-array format.

### Frontend — assets/dashboard.js
- **Panel subtitle — generation timestamp:** Subtitle now shows `"4 active · AI-generated · as of HH:MM · loaded HH:MM TZ"` where `as of` is the signal generation time (converted to local), and `loaded` is the browser fetch time.
- **Stale indicator:** If signals are older than 2 hours, subtitle appends `· stale` in `var(--text3)` color — discrete, no emoji.
- **Backward compat:** Reads both old plain-array and new object format from `signals.json`.

---

## v7.29.5 (2026-04-20) — Intelligent retry when post-filter signals < 4

### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- **Retry logic on under-count:** When Python filters (Rule 5 + Rule 11) reduce the signal count below 4, the script now makes a targeted second Groq call instead of silently accepting 3 signals. The retry prompt tells the model exactly which signals were dropped and why, which pairs are already covered, and how many valid replacements are needed. The retry result is also validated by Rule 5 and Rule 11 before being accepted.
- **Last-resort fallback preserved:** If both the initial call and the retry leave fewer than 3 signals, the unfiltered set is used (same behavior as before).
- **Dropped reasons log:** `dropped_reasons` list now collects human-readable reasons for each filtered signal, used verbatim in the retry prompt for context.

---

## v7.29.4 (2026-04-20) — Python-side signal validation (Rule 5 + Rule 11 guards) + text truncation fix

### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- **Python guard — Rule 5 (COT Extremes):** Added `_is_neutral_cot_signal()` post-processing filter. After Groq returns the signals JSON, any WARN or CRIT signal whose text contains neutral-positioning language (`"neutral positioning"`, `"flat positioning"`, or `long%=4X/5X%`) is dropped before writing to index.json. Prints `INFO Rule-5 filter: dropped...` when fired. Solves the recurring EUR/USD WARN with `long%=50.0%` that the prompt alone could not prevent.
- **Python guard — Rule 11 (No Duplicate Pairs):** Added `_pair_key()` extractor and `seen_pairs` set. For each signal, the FX pair is extracted from the title prefix (e.g. `AUD/USD`). If the same pair already appeared in a higher-priority signal, the duplicate is dropped. Prints `INFO Rule-11 filter: dropped duplicate-pair signal...` when fired.
- **Fallback safety:** If both filters prune the result to fewer than 3 signals (over-pruning edge case), the unfiltered set is used and a WARNING is logged.
- **Bug fix — text truncation:** `signal_text` limit raised from 300 to 600 chars. Institutional multi-layer signals (COT + Vol + price action + watch level) were being cut mid-sentence at 300 chars, visible in the expanded body of the Alerts panel.
- All 4 test cases pass against the signals from the last failing run.

---

## v7.29.3 (2026-04-20) — Anti-duplicate rules + COT extreme guardrails

### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- **Rule 5 — COT Extremes (strengthened):** Added explicit WRONG example for the near-zero net + 50% long% case (the exact pattern that produced a spurious EUR/USD WARN in the previous run). Rule now instructs the model to skip the pair entirely or wait for a multi-layer reason before including it when positioning is neutral.
- **Rule 11 — No Duplicate Pairs (new):** Each FX pair may appear in at most one signal. When multiple reasons exist for the same pair (e.g., AUD/USD positioning + AUD/USD retail sentiment), the model must merge them into a single multi-layer signal or drop the weaker one. The retail layer is only added when it diverges from COT direction (Rule 1 still applies).
- **Rule 12 — Pair Coverage Diversity (new):** Across 4–5 signals, at least 3 different FX pairs must be covered.

---

## v7.29.2 (2026-04-20) — Institutional signal structure + CB differential fix

### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- **Prompt rewrite — SIGNALS_SYSTEM:** Added explicit 5-element Signal Anatomy section (Raw Data → Context → Signal → Implication → Watch Level), matching the standard used in tier-1 bank FX desk notes. Multi-layer signals now aim for 3-5 elements. Simple technical signals may use 1-3.
- **Bug fix — Rule 2 CB Differential:** Added explicit WRONG example with inverted beneficiary ("175bp spread supports EUR" when Fed > ECB). Rule now states the higher-rate currency is supported by carry and provides both CORRECT and WRONG examples covering the Fed > ECB case.
- **Rule 7 language:** Added "do not end with 'worth watching' or 'to monitor'" — signals must close with the implication or watch level.
- **Rule 9 correlation:** Expanded CORRECT example to include the full implication ("risk-off flows redirecting to CHF and Gold rather than JPY").
- **Rule 10 examples:** Replaced short examples with full institutional-depth examples (COT + Vol + price 3-layer; geopolitical + COT + carry; CB + yield + technical) showing all 5 anatomy elements.
- **max_tokens raised to 1100** for signals call (from 900) to accommodate richer signal text.

---

## v7.29.1 (2026-04-20) — Fix signals JSON truncation + narrative direction style

### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- **Bug fix:** `generate_signals` `max_tokens` raised from 600 to 900 — multi-layer signal text (100–250 chars) + evidence was exceeding the budget and truncating the JSON array, causing a parse error and falling back to preserved signals. 
- **Bug fix:** Added JSON parse retry in `generate_signals`: on `JSONDecodeError`, retries once with a brevity reminder prepended to the prompt (text ≤180 chars, evidence ≤2 items). Only raises if the retry also fails.
- **Prompt fix — NARRATIVE_SYSTEM Rule 3:** Restored directional word guidance. The previous version caused the LLM to drop direction words entirely ("gold -0.93%"). New rule: always use a direction word inferred from economic context ("safe-haven bid → gold higher"); use neutral "gold {{gold_pct}}" only when direction is genuinely ambiguous. `fix_direction_mismatch()` remains as the safety net for any residual mismatches.

---

## v7.29.0 (2026-04-20) — Collapsible signal rows, multi-layer prompt, direction-mismatch fix

### Frontend — assets/dashboard.js
- **Change:** Signal rows now render collapsed by default, showing only the timestamp, priority dot, and title. The full body text and evidence chips expand on click. Previously all content was visible inline, making the panel dense to scan.
- **Implementation:** Introduced `.a-body` wrapper for body content (text + evidence chips). Rows with body content get class `a-has-body`; clicking toggles `a-body-open` on the row. Unified under single toggle, replacing the legacy `a-has-ev` / `a-evidence-open` pattern.

### Frontend — assets/dashboard.css
- **Change:** Replaced `.a-has-ev` / `.a-evidence` collapsed pattern with `.a-has-body` / `.a-body` / `.a-body-open`. Title row is `align-items: center` for clean single-line appearance. Body block has left-padding aligned under the title text. `▸` chevron rotates 90° on expand.

### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- **Bug fix:** Added `fix_direction_mismatch()` — post-processes the narrative after `resolve_tokens()` to correct semantic contradictions where the LLM writes a directional word ("higher", "lower", "firms", "retreats") that is inconsistent with the resolved token sign (e.g. "gold higher -0.91%" → "gold lower -0.91%"). Covers gold, equities, and DXY. Runs after `resolve_tokens()` in `_parse_and_process()`. 7/7 unit tests pass.
- **Prompt — NARRATIVE_SYSTEM Rule 3:** Added direction-consistency guidance: LLM is now explicitly instructed that the word before a token must match the token's sign, and that direction-neutral phrasing (`"gold {{gold_pct}}"`) is always safe. "gold higher {{gold_pct}}" is now flagged as WRONG in the prompt.
- **Prompt — SIGNALS_SYSTEM Rule 10:** Raised multi-layer signal minimum from 1 to 2 per run. Added richer CORRECT examples for critical signals (geopolitical + COT + price action for USD/JPY) and wrong examples that call out single-source technical-only signals. Same multi-layer requirement updated in `SIGNALS_SYSTEM_CLOSED`.

### Engine — .github/workflows/generate-ai-narrative.yml
- **Change:** Manual trigger (`workflow_dispatch`) now passes `--force` to the script, bypassing change detection and always calling Groq for fresh signals. Scheduled runs continue using change detection as before.

---

## v7.28.6 (2026-04-20) — Fix FWD RATE always showing — in CB Rates modal

### Frontend — assets/dashboard.js
- **Root cause:** `openCBRatesModal` receives `meetingData` from `window._STATE_meetings[ccy]`, which is set directly from `meetings.json`. That JSON only contains `cutProb`/`hikeProb` — not a `fwdRate` field. The modal's `fwdRate = meetingData?.fwdRate ?? null` therefore always resolved to `null`, causing "—" to display regardless of CB state.
- **Fix:** Inside the `fetchCBExpectations()` currencies loop, after `fwdDisplay` is computed (priority 1 OIS / priority 2 prob-weighted / priority 3 naive step), the result is injected back into `meetingsRes.meetings[ccy].fwdRate`. Since `_STATE_meetings` is set from `meetingsRes.meetings` immediately after the loop, every subsequent modal click receives the correct implied rate string (e.g. `"4.25%"` or `"~4.50%"`). The modal's `parseFloat(fwdRate)` chart path handles the `~` prefix and `%` suffix correctly.

---

## v7.28.5 (2026-04-20) — Remove Gmail SMTP steps from monitor-data-health workflow

### CI — .github/workflows/monitor-data-health.yml
- **Removed `Send email notification on failures` step:** GitHub natively emails the repo owner on workflow failure — the custom Gmail step was redundant and was causing error 535 (App Password required). Removed entirely.
- **Removed `Send weekly digest` step:** Monday-only email digest eliminated along with its cron entry (`0 11 * * 1`). The Job Summary in GitHub Actions already shows the full health report visually.
- **Simplified schedule:** Now a single daily cron (`0 11 * * *`). The Monday-only cron existed solely for the weekly digest.
- **Secrets `NOTIFY_EMAIL_FROM`, `NOTIFY_EMAIL_PASSWORD`, `NOTIFY_EMAIL_TO` no longer referenced** — can be deleted from engine repo → Settings → Secrets and variables → Actions.

## v7.28.1 (2026-04-20) — Rewrite narrative and signals prompts — rule-first, compact structure

### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- **Prompt rewrite — NARRATIVE_SYSTEM:** Reduced from 13,296 → 3,574 chars (~3,300 → ~900 tokens). New structure: numbered mandatory rules at the top ordered by observed violation frequency (TIER-1 subordination → causality missing → hardcoded tokens → DXY with number → CB differential without rates), each with a one-line CORRECT/WRONG example, followed by 3 complete narrative examples.
- **Prompt rewrite — SIGNALS_SYSTEM:** Reduced from 13,510 → 3,556 chars (~3,400 → ~890 tokens). Same restructuring: 9 numbered mandatory rules first ordered by violation frequency (CB differential without rates → BoJ "approaching" → carry inversion → COT false extreme → VIX label → hedge language). The EUR/USD net=-72 long%=50% false extreme from the previous run is explicitly called out as WRONG in rule 5.
- **Net effect:** Total system prompt tokens per run: ~6,700 → ~1,800. The 4 most-violated rules are now at positions 1-3 of each prompt, receiving maximum model attention. Closed-market prompts, validate_regime(), sanitize_hardcoded_tokens(), and all Python logic unchanged.

---

## v7.28.0 (2026-04-19) — Upgrade LLM to llama-4-scout; strengthen narrative and signals prompts with few-shot examples

### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- **Model upgrade:** `llama-3.3-70b-versatile` → `meta-llama/llama-4-scout-17b-16e-instruct`. Scout is Meta's current-generation MoE model on Groq (free tier), with stronger instruction-following on long, structured prompts than llama-3.3-70b. Maverick (128E) was deprecated Feb 2026.
- **Narrative prompt — bad example extended (Opción A):** Added a second annotated bad example drawn directly from observed output failures: subordinating a TIER-1 geopolitical event to "USD mixed", listing pair levels without drivers, and using "yield differential" without naming the specific spread and rate levels. Each violation is annotated with the rule it breaks and the correct form.
- **Narrative prompt — TIER-1 geopolitical examples extended:** Added two concrete correct examples for geopolitical/intelligence-leak events, showing how the lead sentence should name the event, its cross-asset consequence, and the pairs affected — not just note USD direction.
- **Signals prompt — few-shot correct/wrong pairs added (Opción A):** Added `SIGNAL EXAMPLES` block with five annotated correct/wrong pairs covering the most frequent output violations: CB differential without exact rates, BoJ watch framing (160.00 is the watch level, not the current price), RBA carry logic (hiking = supported, not hedged), retail sentiment threshold (≥85% + COT divergence required), and VIX label rule. Each ❌ wrong example matches a real output violation observed in production.

---

## v7.27.10 (2026-04-19) — Remove COT leg2 inversion; show raw CFTC values in cross pair detail

### Frontend — assets/dashboard.js
- **Simplification — COT dual-leg display for crosses:** Removed all quote-leg inversion logic (`cot2Inverted`, `cot2IsFunding`, `FUNDING_CCYS`, `flip2 * -1`, `⁻¹` superscripts, `cotRawNet2`, `lfDir2Raw`). The quote leg (e.g. JPY) now shows the raw CFTC value exactly as the COT Positioning panel does — JPY −65,389 Short in red. No mental translation required. The previous inversion approach added complexity without adding information and created a persistent label/color mismatch that confused users.
- **"aligned" logic preserved and correct:** GBP Long + JPY Short = aligned (both favour GBP/JPY bullish). The aligned/diverging determination now uses raw values consistently for both legs.
- **This file incorporates all changes from v7.27.7 through v7.27.10.**

---

## v7.27.9 (2026-04-19) — Fix COT summary tag label color for quote leg in crosses

### Frontend — assets/dashboard.js
- **Bug fix — summary tag color for leg2:** The `${cotCcy2} LF` label in the cross COT summary tag was colored using `clsI(cotNet2)` — the inverted value. For GBP/JPY, `cotNet2` (JPY inverted) = +65,309 = green, so the label showed "JPY LF Short" in green — contradictory. Fixed: color now uses `clsI(cotRawNet2)` (the raw CFTC value), so "JPY LF Short" renders in red, consistent with the actual CFTC reported position and matching the CFTC Positioning panel.
- **Note:** The zips uploaded in this session predate v7.27.7 and v7.27.8. This file incorporates all three fixes (v7.27.7 + v7.27.8 + v7.27.9) cumulatively.

---

## v7.27.8 (2026-04-19) — Extend COT leg2 inversion to all crosses, not only JPY/CHF

### Frontend — assets/dashboard.js
- **Bug fix — COT leg2 inversion scope:** `cot2Inverted` was `true` only for JPY and CHF as quote currencies. For all other crosses (EUR/GBP, EUR/CAD, AUD/NZD, GBP/AUD, etc.) the quote COT was displayed uninverted — GBP Long (+28,426 in green) in EUR/GBP incorrectly implied bullish EUR/GBP when it is bearish. The fix: quote leg is now always inverted for all crosses (`cot2Inverted = isCross`), because short quote = pair goes up is a universal relationship for any cross, not a JPY/CHF-specific one.
- **Refactor — `cot2IsFunding`:** Introduced to distinguish JPY/CHF as funding/safe-haven currencies (used for the more specific funding-currency tooltip). The `⁻¹` superscript now shows on all cross quote legs (not just funding currencies) to consistently signal the inversion.
- **No change to summary tag logic:** The `lfDir2Raw` / `leg2Aligned` fix from v7.27.7 is unaffected and continues to correctly derive labels from raw CFTC values.

---

## v7.27.7 (2026-04-19) — Fix COT summary tag showing inverted label for funding currencies in crosses

### Frontend — assets/dashboard.js
- **Bug fix — COT summary tag label for JPY/CHF:** `lfDir2` in the summary tag footer was derived from `cotNet2`, which is already multiplied by `−1` for funding currencies (JPY, CHF) to show the cross-perspective value. This caused the tag to display "JPY LF Long" when JPY was net short in the CFTC data — the opposite of what the CFTC table shows. Fixed by introducing `lfDir2Raw` derived from `cotRaw2.net` (the unmodified CFTC value) for the label, while keeping `cotNet2` (inverted) for the numeric display and colour. The `⁻¹` superscript in the metric label already signals the inversion; the summary label now correctly reflects the actual reported CFTC position.
- **Bug fix — "aligned" logic for crosses:** The previous `leg2Aligned = lfDir === lfDir2` comparison was checking two cross-perspective values and calling them "aligned" when both showed `Long` in the display — which for an inverted quote currency (e.g. JPY) means the legs are actually diverging. Fixed: "aligned" now means base LF Long + quote LF Short (or base Short + quote Long), which correctly represents both legs favouring the same directional bias for the cross.

---

## v7.27.6 (2026-04-19) — COT dual-leg display for crosses in buildInlineDetail

### Frontend — assets/dashboard.js
- **Dual-leg COT for crosses:** `buildInlineDetail()` now calculates and renders two independent COT datasets for cross pairs (any pair where neither currency is USD). Previously, only the base currency leg was shown — for GBP/JPY this meant only GBP COT appeared, omitting the JPY leg entirely despite it being structurally essential for reading the cross's bias.
- **Second leg variables added:** `cotCcy2`, `cotRaw2`, `cotNet2`, `cotAmNet2`, `cotWow2`, `cotPctOI2` — fetched from `COT_DATA_CACHE` using the quote currency. No additional network requests; cache was already populated for all 8 G8 currencies at boot.
- **Funding currency inversion:** JPY and CHF are defined in `FUNDING_CCYS`. For these quote currencies, all COT values are multiplied by `-1` before display (`cot2Inverted = true`), so the sign always reads from the cross's perspective — net short JPY futures = bullish GBP/JPY, shown as positive. Labels carry a `⁻¹` superscript and tooltips explain the inversion convention.
- **COT summary tag updated for crosses:** Shows `GBP LF Long · JPY LF Short · diverging` style summary instead of the single-leg `LF Long · AM Short · diverging` format. Alignment is computed between the two base-direction readings.
- **USD pairs unchanged:** The single-leg display (LF Net, LF WoW Δ, AM Net, Net % OI) is preserved exactly for all majors. The `isCross` flag gates the new path.

---

## v7.27.5 (2026-04-19) — Fix: 1W Chg extended to all FX crosses in fetch_intraday_quotes.py

### Engine — scripts/fetch_intraday_quotes.py
- **`FX_MAJORS_1W` restriction removed.** The prior-Friday-close 1W change calculation was gated behind a hardcoded set of 7 major pairs (`eurusd`, `gbpusd`, `usdjpy`, `audusd`, `usdchf`, `usdcad`, `nzdusd`). All 21 crosses (EUR/GBP, GBP/JPY, AUD/JPY, etc.) had `pct1w: null` in `quotes.json`, causing the 1W Chg field to show `—` in the sidebar inline detail expand.
- **Fix:** Removed the `if internal_id in FX_MAJORS_1W` guard. The `try/except` block now runs unconditionally for every FX pair that reaches that code path. Crosses already have the required daily history available at that point (downloaded for HV30 computation) — no additional API calls needed.
- **Convention unchanged:** The calculation method is unchanged — prior-Friday close, Bloomberg/Refinitiv standard. The fix only extends its coverage from 7 to 28 pairs.
- **`[1W]` log lines** will now appear for all crosses in the workflow output.

---

## v7.27.4 (2026-04-19) — UX: sidebar crosses detail replaced popover with inline expand

### Frontend — assets/dashboard.js
- **`toggleSidebarDetail(row, tvSym)` added:** New function that handles double-click on sidebar cross rows. Inserts a `.sb-expand-row > .sb-expand-inner` div immediately below the selected `.sb-row`, animates open to `max-height: 160px`, and populates it by calling the existing `buildInlineDetail()` — the same function used by the FX Pairs table expand. Toggle behaviour mirrors `toggleInlineDetail`: double-clicking an open row collapses it; double-clicking a different row collapses the current one and opens the new one.
- **Sidebar `dblclick` handler updated:** Previously called `openPairPopover()` — now calls `toggleSidebarDetail()`. The floating popover (`openPairPopover`, `closePairPopover`, `updatePairDetail`) is unchanged and remains available for other contexts.
- **`buildInlineDetail()` unchanged:** No modifications — reused as-is, which guarantees the sidebar detail strip is byte-for-byte identical to the FX Pairs table expand in content, formatting, tooltips, and data sources.

### Frontend — assets/dashboard.css
- **`.sb-row.sb-selected`:** Applies `var(--bg3)` background and a `▾` indicator on `.sb-sym::after` — mirrors the `tr.pd-selected` treatment in the FX Pairs table.
- **`.sb-expand-row`:** Block-level container with `var(--bg2)` background and `border-bottom: 1px solid var(--border2)`. Wraps the inner animated div.
- **`.sb-expand-inner`:** `max-height: 0` default, `overflow: hidden`, `transition: max-height .18s ease`. JS sets `max-height: 160px` on open — same animation budget as `tr.pd-expand-row`.

### Frontend — index.html
- **Version string bumped:** v7.27.2 → v7.27.3.

---

## v7.27.3 (2026-04-19) — Market Research audit: GBP/JPY page label, Bloomberg attribution, investment advice language

### Market Research — guide-gbpjpy-cot.html

**Problem 1 — Page presented as "Guide" throughout despite being classified as Market Research.**
- **`<title>`** updated: "GBP/JPY Bias Guide: …" → "GBP/JPY Bias Factors: …"
- **`og:title`** updated to match.
- **`twitter:title`** updated to match.
- **`page-hero-label`** (visible label below the topbar): "Guide" → "Market Research".
- **`aria-label` on `<nav>`**: "Guide sections" → "Market Research sections".
- **Statusbar `PAGE` label**: "GUIDE · GBP/JPY" → "MARKET RESEARCH · GBP/JPY".

**Problem 2 — Bloomberg attribution on Net%OI column description.**
- Removed "the Bloomberg-standard normalization" — replaced with "the standard institutional normalization for COT data". The Net%OI convention is widely used across institutional platforms; attributing it exclusively to Bloomberg is unverifiable and unnecessary.

**Problem 3 — Investment advice language in six locations, all rewritten to descriptive framing per GUIDELINES.**
- **RISK-ON regime bullet:** "Net short JPY positions *can be sustained and extended*" → "Net short JPY positions *historically persist and extend* in this regime." Removes the implication of a recommended course of action.
- **MIXED regime bullet:** "*Expect* choppy behavior … *Wait for regime clarification*" → "*Range-bound behavior without a strong catalyst is the historical pattern* in this regime." Removes the directive.
- **CAUTION regime bullet:** "*Reduce confidence in medium-term GBP/JPY longs*" → "*COT directional signals are historically less reliable in this environment.*" Removes explicit position-sizing instruction.
- **RISK-OFF regime bullet:** "*Do not fight this regime*" → "GBP/JPY *has historically dropped* hundreds of pips in a single session *during this regime*." Removes the trading directive; preserves the empirical observation.
- **Retail sentiment table:** "*likely chasing or bag-holding a rally*" → "*accumulated into a rally*"; "*likely fading a downtrend or positioned for a reversal*" → "*accumulated into a decline*". Retail trading jargon replaced with neutral descriptive language.
- **Multi-factor framework intro:** "*High-conviction GBP/JPY reads require alignment across at least three of the four factors. Use this framework to score the current environment*" → "*Cross-referencing all four factors produces a more reliable directional read than any single signal in isolation.*" Removes the scoring-for-action framing.
- **Strongest setup callout:** "*Strongest setup — all factors aligned bullish*" → "*Maximum factor alignment — all signals pointing in the same direction*"; "*Three-of-four alignment is sufficient for a directional bias; fewer than three warrants staying neutral*" → "*Alignment across three or more factors produces a stronger directional read than one or two factors in isolation.*" Removes the operational trading rule; preserves the analytical observation.

### Version
- **Version string bumped:** v7.27.0 → v7.27.3.

## v7.27.2 (2026-04-19) — SEO: sitemap completed, robots.txt zombie block, guide-cross-asset expanded

### Guides — guide-cross-asset-risk.html
- **New section: Regime Episodes — Cross-Asset Flow Relationships.** Four documented market regimes with per-indicator behavior tables and FX implications: the 2022 rates shock (simultaneous equity/bond selloff, MOVE sustained above 130, EUR/USD parity break), the 2023 Q1 regional banking stress (MOVE spike to 180 vs. VIX at 26 — MOVE/VIX divergence as the critical diagnostic), the 2024 August JPY carry unwind (VIX intraday spike to 65, Nikkei -12.4%, BTC as leading indicator preceding the Nikkei open), and the 2025 Q1 tariff shock (gold/DXY positive correlation, EUR/USD rally from 1.03 to 1.12). Each episode includes a diagnostic callout identifying the cross-asset signature that distinguished the regime type. Targets the high-impression query "cross-asset flow relationships risk rates fx commodities crypto" (113 impressions, pos. 5.9, CTR 0% in the 22 Mar–18 Apr 2026 GSC period).
- **New section: Crypto as a Cross-Asset Risk Indicator.** Covers the mechanism by which BTC functions as a leading Asia session risk proxy, the structural BTC/AUD positive correlation and how to read it from the Correlations table, the Asia session timing window (18:00–02:00 UTC) where BTC is the primary continuously traded risk asset, a flow table mapping BTC behavior to FX pair implications, and four documented conditions under which the indicator breaks down (crypto-specific catalysts, weekend gaps, late-cycle carry regimes, crisis-level VIX above 40). Framed institutionally — BTC as one of several Asia session risk monitors alongside Nikkei futures and CME Globex, not a standalone signal.
- **TOC updated:** Two new entries added — "Regime Episodes" and "Crypto as Risk Indicator" — before the existing toc-divider.
- **Version string bumped:** v7.27.0 → v7.27.2.

### SEO — sitemap.xml
- **`guide-gbpjpy-cot.html` added to sitemap** under a new `<!-- Market Research -->` comment block, priority 0.8, changefreq monthly. Page was present in the repo and generating Search Console impressions but was absent from the sitemap, preventing Google from discovering its priority signal.
- **`guide-cot.html` priority bumped** from 0.8 to 0.85. Highest-traffic guide by impression volume (1,584 impressions in the 22 Mar – 18 Apr 2026 period); priority now reflects its relative importance within the guide set.
- **All `lastmod` dates updated** from 2026-04-17 to 2026-04-19 to reflect the current deploy.

### SEO — robots.txt
- **Disallow directives added** for `news.html`, `en.html`, `guia-cot.html`, and `tecnico-vs-fundamental.html` under both `User-agent: *` and `User-agent: Googlebot`. These pages are deleted from the codebase but remain indexed by Google, consuming crawl budget and fragmenting domain authority. The Disallow directives stop future crawling; the existing `404.html` redirect map handles inbound links to these URLs. Manual deindexation via Search Console (Removals tool) is recommended to accelerate removal from the index.

### Frontend — index.html
- **Version string bumped** from v7.26.3 to v7.27.2 in the statusbar.

## v7.26.4 (2026-04-19) — AI narrative: change detection skips redundant Groq calls

### Engine — scripts/generate_narrative_signals.py
- **Change detection added (`build_snapshot`, `market_data_changed`, `load_snapshot`, `save_snapshot`):** Before calling Groq, the script now builds a snapshot of analytically significant market fields and compares it against the snapshot saved by the previous run. Groq is only called when something material has changed. Fields compared: 7 FX pair prices (threshold: 20 pips for majors, 0.20 for JPY), 8 cross-asset values (VIX ±0.50, SPX ±20pts, Gold ±$15, DXY ±0.15, MOVE ±3, yields ±3bp), COT `weekEnding` per currency (any new CFTC report forces regeneration), and high-impact news headlines (any new or removed headline forces regeneration).
- **Skip behaviour:** When unchanged, `checked_at` is updated in `index.json` (so the frontend knows the run completed) but no Groq call is made and the existing narrative and signals are preserved. Prints `⏭ Context unchanged (reason) — skipping Groq.`
- **Weekend skip:** When market is closed and the previous run was also a closed-market run, the script exits immediately — weekend prices are static Friday closing levels and no new data arrives between runs.
- **Force override:** `--force` flag bypasses change detection for manual testing or forced refresh.
- **Snapshot file:** `ai-analysis/context_snapshot.json` — committed to the public repo alongside `index.json` and `signals.json` so the snapshot persists across workflow runs.
- **Expected token savings:** At 12 runs/day, market data is materially unchanged for roughly 6–8 consecutive runs during quiet sessions (Asia overnight, weekend). Estimated reduction: 50–65% fewer Groq calls, from ~43,200 tokens/day to ~15,000–22,000 tokens/day across 3 keys.

### Engine — .github/workflows/generate-ai-narrative.yml
- **`git add` updated:** `ai-analysis/context_snapshot.json` added to the commit step so the snapshot persists between workflow runs.

---
## v7.26.3 (2026-04-19) — PWA: Service Worker, Web App Manifest, installability

### Frontend — index.html
- **PWA meta tags added:** `<link rel="manifest" href="/manifest.json">`, `mobile-web-app-capable`, `apple-mobile-web-app-capable`, `apple-mobile-web-app-status-bar-style` and `apple-mobile-web-app-title` inserted after `theme-color` in the SEO Core block. The duplicate `<link rel="apple-touch-icon">` that had been added in the SEO block (already present in the Favicon block) was removed.
- **Inline SW script replaced:** Service Worker registration was initially implemented as an inline `<script>` block in `<head>`, violating the pre-deploy checklist ("no inline CSS/JS blocks"). Extracted to `assets/sw-register.js` and loaded with `<script src="assets/sw-register.js" defer></script>`.
- **Cache-bust versions bumped:** `dashboard.css?v=7.26.2` → `?v=7.26.3`, `dashboard.js?v=7.26.2` → `?v=7.26.3`.
- **Statusbar updated:** `FX Terminal v7.26.2` → `FX Terminal v7.26.3`.

### Frontend — assets/sw-register.js (new file)
- **Service Worker registration module:** Registers `/sw.js` on `window load`. Wrapped in `'serviceWorker' in navigator` guard for graceful degradation. Errors caught with `console.warn` only — registration failure is non-fatal and does not affect the terminal's core functionality.

### Frontend — sw.js (new file, site root)
- **Service Worker:** Cache-first strategy for the static shell (HTML, CSS, JS, icons, manifest); network-first with cache fallback for all JSON data directories (`/ai-analysis/`, `/cot-data/`, `/economic-data/`, `/extended-data/`, `/fx-data/`, `/intraday-data/`, `/meetings-data/`, `/news-data/`, `/rates/`, `/rr-data/`, `/sentiment-data/`). Cross-origin requests and non-GET methods pass through unhandled. Cache versioned as `gi-v1`; old caches pruned on activate.

### Frontend — manifest.json (new file, site root)
- **Web App Manifest:** Enables installability on supporting browsers. `display: standalone`, `orientation: landscape-primary` (appropriate for a terminal layout). Icons: `favicon-192x192.png` (192×192, maskable) and `apple-touch-icon.png` (180×180). `background_color` and `theme_color` both `#0d1117` (canonical terminal background). `start_url: /`.

### Frontend — assets/dashboard.js
- **UI state persistence module added (`initUIStatePersistence`):** Saves and restores the last chart symbol and last visited section across sessions using `localStorage` key `gi_ui_state`. On load, the saved symbol is restored after an 800ms delay (letting the initial boot chart render first) and the saved section is scrolled into view after 1000ms. `loadTVChart` is wrapped so every symbol change is intercepted and persisted without modifying the original function. Split layout state is handled separately by `initSplitLayout()` and is not duplicated here.
- **COT digest notification module added (`initCOTDigestNotification`):** After page load, fetches `cot-data/digest.json` and fires a Service Worker push notification if `Notification.permission === 'granted'` and `digest.weekEnding` is newer than `localStorage` key `gi_cot_notif_seen`. Uses `tag: 'gi-cot-{weekEnding}'` to prevent duplicate notifications on tab reload. Clicking the notification opens/focuses the terminal at `#section-positioning`. Does not request notification permission — that remains exclusively the onboarding tooltip's responsibility.

### Engine — .github/workflows/update-cot-cftc-all.yml
- **New step: "Generate COT digest for push notification":** Runs after all 8 currency files are saved, before the git commit. Reads each `cot-data/{CCY}.json`, calculates WoW delta, sorts by absolute magnitude, and writes `cot-data/digest.json` with: `weekEnding`, `topCcy`, `topWow`, `topWowStr`, `topNet`, `topNetPct`, `message`, and `summary` (all 8 currencies). Message format: `"CAD shorts increased 15.7k contracts · NET -63.6k (-60.9%)"` — absolute WoW magnitude, direction conveyed by "longs/shorts increased".

### Frontend — sw.js
- **`notificationclick` handler added:** Focuses an existing terminal tab or opens a new one when user taps the COT digest notification.

---
## v7.26.2 (2026-04-19) — Onboarding tooltip: first-visit alert discovery

### UX — Retention
- **First-visit onboarding tooltip added (`gi-onboard`):** New users who have no alerts configured and have not previously dismissed the tooltip see a non-intrusive card in the bottom-right corner after a 4-second delay. The card explains the RISK-OFF regime alert in plain language and offers a single "SET ALERT" button.
- **"SET ALERT" action:** Requests browser `Notification` permission, adds a `REGIME → RISK-OFF` alert to `localStorage` (`gi_alerts`), opens the alerts popover briefly so the user sees the alert was registered, then dismisses the tooltip. Zero friction — one click from awareness to active alert.
- **Auto-dismiss:** Tooltip fades out automatically after 18 seconds if ignored, avoiding persistent visual clutter.
- **Permanent dismissal:** Both "Maybe later" and "SET ALERT" write `gi_ob_done=1` to `localStorage` so the tooltip never reappears on subsequent visits.
- **Guard conditions:** Tooltip is suppressed if the user already has any alert configured or has dismissed it before — existing users are never interrupted.
- **Motivation:** GA retention data (cohort week 5 Apr–11 Apr: 27 new users, 0 returned week 1) identified that the alerts feature was not being discovered. The REGIME alert is the primary retention mechanism — a user who activates it has a concrete reason to return.

---
